text: I know Mademoiselle Cynthia. It is by the charity of that good Mrs. Inglethorp that I am here.
context: Poirot explains to Hastings and Cynthia why he and the Belgian refugees are living in the village.

text: Yes, my friend, she had kindly extended hospitality to seven of my countrypeople who, alas, are refugees from their native land. We Belgians will always remember her with gratitude.
context: Poirot expresses gratitude for Mrs. Inglethorp's help to Belgian refugees.

text: The mind is confused? Is it not so? Take time, *mon ami*. You are agitated; you are excited—it is but natural. Presently, when we are calmer, we will arrange the facts, neatly, each in his proper place.
context: Poirot calms Hastings before organizing his confused account of the tragedy.

text: Not so. *Voyons!* One fact leads to another—so we continue.
context: Poirot explains that a deduction proceeds by linking one fact to the next.

text: Does the next fit in with that? *A merveille!* Good! We can proceed.
context: Poirot tests whether the next fact fits the developing chain of reasoning.

text: This next little fact—no! Ah, that is curious! There is something missing—a link in the chain that is not there. We examine. We search.
context: Poirot describes noticing a fact that fails to fit the developing explanation.

text: Beware! Peril to the detective who says: 'It is so small—it does not matter. It will not agree. I will forget it.' That way lies confusion! Everything matters.
context: Poirot warns Hastings never to dismiss an inconvenient small detail.

text: And I am pleased with you. You have a good memory, and you have given me the facts faithfully. Of the order in which you present them, I say nothing—truly, it is deplorable! But I make allowances—you are upset.
context: Poirot praises Hastings's memory while teasing him about his disorderly presentation.

text: Now I am ready. We will proceed to the château, and study matters on the spot. Excuse me, *mon ami*, you dressed in haste, and your tie is on one side. Permit me.
context: Before leaving, Poirot combines investigative purpose with concern for Hastings's crooked tie.

text: it is not as though there was a blood tie. She has been kind and generous to these Cavendishes, but she was not their own mother. Blood tells—always remember that—blood tells.
context: Poirot interprets the family's restrained grief after Mrs. Inglethorp's death.

text: I do not mind telling you—though, as you know, it is not my habit to explain until the end is reached. The present contention is that Mrs. Inglethorp died of strychnine poisoning, presumably administered in her coffee.
context: Poirot reluctantly begins explaining his concern about the timing of the poison.

text: Therefore she drank it between then and half-past eight—certainly not much later. Well, strychnine is a fairly rapid poison. Its effects would be felt very soon, probably in about an hour. Yet, in Mrs. Inglethorp's case, the symptoms do not manifest themselves until five o'clock the next morning: nine hours!
context: Poirot compares the coffee time with the delayed onset of symptoms.

text: But a heavy meal, taken at about the same time as the poison, might retard its effects, though hardly to that extent. Still, it is a possibility to be taken into account. But, according to you, she ate very little for supper, and yet the symptoms do not develop until early the next morning! Now that is a curious circumstance, my friend.
context: Poirot evaluates and rejects a heavy meal as a sufficient explanation for the delay.

text: Foot-marks? But what an idea! There has already been practically an army in the room! What foot-marks are we likely to find? No, come here and aid me in my search.
context: Poirot dismisses Hastings's concern about preserving footprints after many people entered the room.

text: You do not? Observe the lamp—the chimney is broken in two places; they lie there as they fell. But see, the coffee-cup is absolutely smashed to powder.
context: Poirot contrasts the naturally broken lamp with the pulverized coffee cup.

text: somebody stepped on that cup, grinding it to powder, and the reason they did so was either because it contained strychnine or—which is far more serious—because it did not contain strychnine!
context: Poirot explains the two possible motives for destroying the coffee cup.

text: I have no authority to go through these papers. But it should be done—at once!
context: Poirot refuses to inspect the victim's papers without authority while stressing their urgency.

text: I have no authority to go through these papers. But it should be done—at once!

text: It must have been done since yesterday, otherwise a good housemaid would have at once removed it with blotting-paper and a hot iron. One of my best hats once—but that is not to the point.
context: Poirot dates the candle grease from housekeeping practice and digresses about a damaged hat.

text: but it was not his candle that made this great patch, for you perceive that this is white grease; whereas Monsieur Lawrence's candle, which is still on the dressing-table, is pink. On the other hand, Mrs. Inglethorp had no candlestick in the room, only a reading-lamp.
context: Poirot uses the colour of the wax to exclude Lawrence's candle.

text: Admirable! What symmetry! Observe that crescent; and those diamonds—their neatness rejoices the eye. The spacing of the plants, also, is perfect. It has been recently done; is it not so?
context: Poirot pauses to admire the geometrical flower beds outside the boudoir.

text: *Eh bien, eh bien!* Do not grudge me a moment's satisfaction of the eye.
context: Poirot asks Hastings to let him enjoy the flower beds before interviewing Dorcas.

text: And how do you know that these fine begonias are not of equal importance?
context: Poirot challenges Hastings's assumption that the flower beds are irrelevant.

text: My good Dorcas, it is necessary that I should know every detail of that quarrel as fully as possible. Do not think that you are betraying your mistress's secrets. Your mistress lies dead, and it is necessary that we should know all—if we are to avenge her.
context: Poirot persuades Dorcas that revealing the quarrel is a duty to the dead woman.

text: Probably not, but do you notice anything that strikes you as peculiar about this box?
context: Poirot asks Hastings to inspect the empty sleeping-powder box.

text: Yet the explanation is quite simple. So do not intrigue yourself, my friend.
context: Poirot tempers Hastings's excitement about the unlabelled powder box.

text: You are not pleased with me, *mon ami?*
context: Poirot notices Hastings's irritation after rejecting his cocoa theory.

text: *Ne vous fâchez pas!* Allow me to interest myself in my coffee-cups, and I will respect your cocoa. There! Is it a bargain?
context: Poirot humorously makes peace with Hastings over competing theories.

text: So Mrs. Cavendish stood by the tray—and poured out. Yes. Then she came across to the window where you sat with Mademoiselle Cynthia. Yes. Here are the three cups.
context: Poirot reconstructs the movements around the coffee tray.

text: It is evident! I had an idea—but clearly I was mistaken. Yes, altogether I was mistaken. Yet it is strange. But no matter!
context: Poirot reacts to testing the coffee cups and temporarily rejects an idea.

text: But I am most serious. Ah, there is something there that I do not understand. My instinct was right.
context: Poirot insists that Cynthia's coffee habit confirms an unresolved instinct.

text: The instinct that led me to insist on examining those coffee-cups. *Chut!* no more now!
context: Poirot explains his instinct and abruptly ends the discussion.

text: A scribbled over old envelope, and a freshly planted bed of begonias.
context: Poirot names the two clues that led him to the newly made will.

text: Ah, I am glad she has come. There is a woman with a head and a heart too, Hastings. Though the good God gave her no beauty!
context: Poirot assesses Evelyn Howard when she returns to Styles.

text: But I must ask you to trust me. Now your help may be very valuable to me. I will tell you why. Because, in all this house of mourning, yours are the only eyes that have wept.
context: Poirot appeals to Miss Howard's genuine grief and asks for her trust.

text: Who? That is the question. Why? Ah, if I only knew.
context: Poirot separates the unknown identity and motive behind the forced lock.

text: There was something in that case—some piece of evidence, slight in itself perhaps, but still enough of a clue to connect the murderer with the crime. It was vital to him that it should be destroyed before it was discovered and its significance appreciated. Therefore, he took the risk, the great risk, of coming in here. Finding the case locked, he was obliged to force it, thus betraying his presence. For him to take that risk, it must have been something of great importance.
context: Poirot infers why the murderer risked forcing the despatch-case.

text: I guessed nothing! I have behaved like an imbecile! I should never have left that case here. I should have carried it away with me. Ah, triple pig!
context: Poirot angrily condemns his own failure to safeguard the evidence.

text: You gave too much rein to your imagination. Imagination is a good servant, and a bad master. The simplest explanation is always the most likely.
context: Poirot explains why Hastings misread the clues about the will.

text: What have I always told you? Everything must be taken into account. If the fact will not fit the theory—let the theory go.
context: Poirot rebukes Hastings for dismissing an inconvenient conversation as unimportant.

text: The case is not clear yet—no. For it is of the most complicated! It puzzles *me*. *Me*, Hercule Poirot! There are two facts of significance.
context: Poirot admits the case challenges even him before identifying two major facts.

text: In all this, you see, I think of that poor Mrs. Inglethorp who is dead. She was not extravagantly loved—no. But she was very good to us Belgians—I owe her a debt.
context: Poirot explains the personal obligation driving his investigation.

text: *Mon Dieu!* I had some warm moments in that court; I did not figure to myself that the man would be so pig-headed as to refuse to say anything at all. Decidedly, it was the policy of an imbecile.
context: Poirot discusses Alfred's disastrous inquest conduct with Hastings.

text: See; say that it is I who have committed this murder, I can think of seven most plausible stories! Far more convincing than Mr. Inglethorp's stony denials!
context: Poirot boasts that even a guilty man could invent a better defence than Alfred's.

text: Real evidence is usually vague and unsatisfactory. It has to be examined—sifted. But here the whole thing is cut and dried.
context: Poirot contrasts genuine evidence with the overly neat case against Alfred.

text: No, my friend, this evidence has been very cleverly manufactured—so cleverly that it has defeated its own ends.
context: Poirot concludes that the evidence against Alfred was deliberately fabricated.

text: Bah! Do not ask me to believe that any man could be so idiotic! Only a lunatic, who wished to commit suicide by causing himself to be hanged, would act so!
context: Poirot rejects the idea that Alfred openly bought poison and created obvious suspicion.

text: I beg your pardon, he saw a man with a black beard like Mr. Inglethorp's, and wearing glasses like Mr. Inglethorp, and dressed in Mr. Inglethorp's rather noticeable clothes.
context: Poirot distinguishes seeing a disguise from recognizing Alfred himself.

text: But in the case of Alfred Inglethorp, all that is changed. His clothes, his beard, the glasses which hide his eyes—those are the salient points about his personal appearance.
context: Poirot explains why Alfred's conspicuous appearance is easy to imitate.

text: when you find that people are not telling you the truth—look out! Now, unless I am much mistaken, at the inquest to-day only one—at most, two persons were speaking the truth without reservation or subterfuge.
context: Poirot warns Hastings that widespread evasiveness signals danger.

text: Was Dr. Bauerstein here on Tuesday evening? Here? And you never told me? Why did you not tell me? Why? Why?
context: Poirot erupts when Hastings reveals Bauerstein visited on the murder night.

text: We know now that there is one person who did not buy the poison. We have cleared away the manufactured clues. Now for the real ones.
context: Poirot reframes the investigation after proving Alfred's alibi.

text: I have been obliged to show my hand sooner than I intended. As long as I might be thought to be pursuing him, the criminal would be off his guard. Now, he will be doubly careful. Yes—doubly careful.
context: Poirot explains the strategic cost of revealing Alfred's innocence.

text: Do not fear. Speak your mind. You should always pay attention to your instincts.
context: Poirot encourages Hastings to voice an apparently foolish suspicion.

text: Still you are right in one thing. It is always wise to suspect everybody until you can prove logically, and to your own satisfaction, that they are innocent.
context: Poirot states his rule of universal provisional suspicion.

text: You argue like a child. If Miss Howard were capable of poisoning the old lady, she would be quite equally capable of simulating devotion. No, we must look elsewhere. You are perfectly correct in your assumption that her vehemence against Alfred Inglethorp is too violent to be natural; but you are quite wrong in the deduction you draw from it. I have drawn my own deductions, which I believe to be correct, but I will not speak of them at present.
context: Poirot rejects Hastings's claim that devotion proves Miss Howard innocent.

text: Next time you happen to be alone with Lawrence Cavendish, I want you to say this to him. 'I have a message for you, from Poirot. He says: "Find the extra coffee-cup, and you can rest in peace!"' Nothing more. Nothing less.
context: Poirot instructs Hastings to deliver an exact cryptic message to Lawrence.

text: You realize that he chose the one place in the house to hide it where its presence would not be remarked? Yes, he is intelligent. But we must be more intelligent. We must be so intelligent that he does not suspect us of being intelligent at all.
context: Poirot admires the intelligence behind hiding the beard among costumes.

text: No. You wish to believe he committed the crime. You believe him capable of committing it. But your instinct tells you he did not commit it.
context: Poirot tells Miss Howard that her instinct contradicts her expressed certainty about Alfred.

text: Shall I tell you why you have been so vehement against Mr. Inglethorp? It is because you have been trying to believe what you wish to believe. It is because you are trying to drown and stifle your instinct, which tells you another name——
context: Poirot interprets Miss Howard's vehemence as an effort to suppress another suspicion.

text: I will ask you nothing. It is enough for me that it is as I thought. And I—I, too, have an instinct. We are working together towards a common end.
context: Poirot accepts Miss Howard's unspoken suspicion without forcing a confession.

text: I am not keeping back facts. Every fact that I know is in your possession. You can draw your own deductions from them. This time it is a question of ideas.
context: Poirot distinguishes shared facts from the private interpretation he withholds from Hastings.

text: My good Dorcas, I have an idea—a little idea—if it should prove justified, what magnificent chance! Tell me, on Monday, not Tuesday, Dorcas, but Monday, the day before the tragedy, did anything go wrong with Mrs. Inglethorp's bell?
context: Poirot tests a new theory by asking about the victim's bell wire.

text: See you, one should not ask for outside proof—no, reason should be enough. But the flesh is weak, it is consolation to find that one is on the right track.
context: Poirot celebrates external confirmation of a deduction about the cut bell wire.

text: Ah, my friend, I am like a giant refreshed. I run! I leap!
context: Poirot exuberantly reacts to proof that his theory is correct.

text: For, see you, it is a big stake for which I play. No one but I, Hercule Poirot, would attempt it!
context: Poirot weighs whether to act and boasts of the audacity of his plan.

text: These little grey cells. It is 'up to them'—as you say over here.
context: Poirot says the case must be solved by mental reasoning.

text: You cannot mix up sentiment and reason.
context: Poirot tells Hastings not to let friendship distort judgment about John.

text: Oh, my friend, have I not said to you all along that I have no proofs. It is one thing to know that a man is guilty, it is quite another matter to prove him so.
context: Poirot distinguishes private conviction from admissible proof after John's arrest.

text: We have to deal with a most clever and unscrupulous man, and we must use any means in our power—otherwise he will slip through our fingers.
context: Poirot justifies strategic deception against a clever murderer.

text: This time it is an idea gigantic! Stupendous! And you—*you*, my friend, have given it to me!
context: A remark by Hastings triggers Poirot's decisive realization.

text: Looking at the matter psychologically, I drew one deduction which I was convinced was correct. The second 'scandal' she spoke of was not the same as the first—and it concerned herself!
context: Poirot distinguishes two different scandals from the victim's changing language.

text: What third medium was there—a medium so suitable for disguising the taste of strychnine that it is extraordinary no one has thought of it?
context: Poirot identifies the victim's medicine as the true vehicle for the poison.

text: Throughout the case, there have been evidences that the tragedy was intended to take place on Monday evening. On that day, Mrs. Inglethorp's bell wire was neatly cut, and on Monday evening Mademoiselle Cynthia was spending the night with friends, so that Mrs. Inglethorp would have been quite alone in the right wing, completely shut off from help of any kind, and would have died, in all probability, before medical aid could have been summoned. But in her hurry to be in time for the village entertainment Mrs. Inglethorp forgot to take her medicine, and the next day she lunched away from home, so that the last—and fatal—dose was actually taken twenty-four hours later than had been anticipated by the murderer; and it is owing to that delay that the final proof—the last link of the chain—is now in my hands.
context: Poirot reconstructs the murderers' original schedule and isolation plan.

text: A letter in the murderer's own hand-writing, *mes amis!* Had it been a little clearer in its terms, it is possible that Mrs. Inglethorp, warned in time, would have escaped.
context: Poirot presents the final documentary proof against the murderer.

text: I did not deceive you, *mon ami*. At most, I permitted you to deceive yourself.
context: Poirot answers Hastings's complaint that he was deliberately misled.

text: You see, my friend, you have a nature so honest, and a countenance so transparent, that—*enfin*, to conceal your feelings is impossible!
context: Poirot explains why Hastings could not be trusted with the true theory.

text: This attitude of his gave me furiously to think, and I was slowly forced to the conclusion that Alfred Inglethorp wanted to be arrested. *Eh bien!* from that moment, I was equally determined that he should not be arrested.
context: Poirot explains his strategic response to Alfred's plan.

text: Because, *mon ami*, it is the law of your country that a man once acquitted can never be tried again for the same offence. Aha! but it was clever—his idea!
context: Poirot reveals Alfred's plan to secure permanent protection through acquittal.

text: And, then, there was that rather over-vehement hatred of hers! It concealed a very opposite emotion. There was, undoubtedly, a tie of passion between them long before he came to Styles.
context: Poirot interprets Miss Howard's hatred as concealment of passion for Alfred.

text: Never mind. Console yourself, my friend. We may hunt together again, who knows?
context: Poirot consoles Hastings over his romantic disappointments and suggests future cases together.

text: It must have been done since yesterday, otherwise a good housemaid would have at once removed it with blotting-paper and a hot iron. One of my best hats once—but that is not to the point.
context: Poirot dates the candle grease from housekeeping practice and digresses about a damaged hat.

text: We know now that there is one person who did not buy the poison. We have cleared away the manufactured clues. Now for the real ones.
context: Poirot reframes the investigation after proving Alfred's alibi.

text: I have been obliged to show my hand sooner than I intended. As long as I might be thought to be pursuing him, the criminal would be off his guard. Now, he will be doubly careful. Yes—doubly careful.
context: Poirot explains the strategic cost of revealing Alfred's innocence.

text: Do not fear. Speak your mind. You should always pay attention to your instincts.
context: Poirot encourages Hastings to voice an apparently foolish suspicion.

text: Still you are right in one thing. It is always wise to suspect everybody until you can prove logically, and to your own satisfaction, that they are innocent.
context: Poirot states his rule of universal provisional suspicion.

text: You argue like a child. If Miss Howard were capable of poisoning the old lady, she would be quite equally capable of simulating devotion. No, we must look elsewhere. You are perfectly correct in your assumption that her vehemence against Alfred Inglethorp is too violent to be natural; but you are quite wrong in the deduction you draw from it. I have drawn my own deductions, which I believe to be correct, but I will not speak of them at present.
context: Poirot rejects Hastings's claim that devotion proves Miss Howard innocent.

text: Next time you happen to be alone with Lawrence Cavendish, I want you to say this to him. 'I have a message for you, from Poirot. He says: "Find the extra coffee-cup, and you can rest in peace!"' Nothing more. Nothing less.
context: Poirot instructs Hastings to deliver an exact cryptic message to Lawrence.

text: You realize that he chose the one place in the house to hide it where its presence would not be remarked? Yes, he is intelligent. But we must be more intelligent. We must be so intelligent that he does not suspect us of being intelligent at all.
context: Poirot admires the intelligence behind hiding the beard among costumes.

text: No. You wish to believe he committed the crime. You believe him capable of committing it. But your instinct tells you he did not commit it.
context: Poirot tells Miss Howard that her instinct contradicts her expressed certainty about Alfred.

text: Shall I tell you why you have been so vehement against Mr. Inglethorp? It is because you have been trying to believe what you wish to believe. It is because you are trying to drown and stifle your instinct, which tells you another name——
context: Poirot interprets Miss Howard's vehemence as an effort to suppress another suspicion.

text: I will ask you nothing. It is enough for me that it is as I thought. And I—I, too, have an instinct. We are working together towards a common end.
context: Poirot accepts Miss Howard's unspoken suspicion without forcing a confession.

text: I am not keeping back facts. Every fact that I know is in your possession. You can draw your own deductions from them. This time it is a question of ideas.
context: Poirot distinguishes shared facts from the private interpretation he withholds from Hastings.

text: My good Dorcas, I have an idea—a little idea—if it should prove justified, what magnificent chance! Tell me, on Monday, not Tuesday, Dorcas, but Monday, the day before the tragedy, did anything go wrong with Mrs. Inglethorp's bell?
context: Poirot tests a new theory by asking about the victim's bell wire.

text: See you, one should not ask for outside proof—no, reason should be enough. But the flesh is weak, it is consolation to find that one is on the right track.
context: Poirot celebrates external confirmation of a deduction about the cut bell wire.

text: Ah, my friend, I am like a giant refreshed. I run! I leap!
context: Poirot exuberantly reacts to proof that his theory is correct.

text: For, see you, it is a big stake for which I play. No one but I, Hercule Poirot, would attempt it!
context: Poirot weighs whether to act and boasts of the audacity of his plan.

text: These little grey cells. It is 'up to them'—as you say over here.
context: Poirot says the case must be solved by mental reasoning.

text: You cannot mix up sentiment and reason.
context: Poirot tells Hastings not to let friendship distort judgment about John.

text: Oh, my friend, have I not said to you all along that I have no proofs. It is one thing to know that a man is guilty, it is quite another matter to prove him so.
context: Poirot distinguishes private conviction from admissible proof after John's arrest.

text: We have to deal with a most clever and unscrupulous man, and we must use any means in our power—otherwise he will slip through our fingers.
context: Poirot justifies strategic deception against a clever murderer.

text: This time it is an idea gigantic! Stupendous! And you—*you*, my friend, have given it to me!
context: A remark by Hastings triggers Poirot's decisive realization.

text: Looking at the matter psychologically, I drew one deduction which I was convinced was correct. The second 'scandal' she spoke of was not the same as the first—and it concerned herself!
context: Poirot distinguishes two different scandals from the victim's changing language.

text: What third medium was there—a medium so suitable for disguising the taste of strychnine that it is extraordinary no one has thought of it?
context: Poirot identifies the victim's medicine as the true vehicle for the poison.

text: Throughout the case, there have been evidences that the tragedy was intended to take place on Monday evening. On that day, Mrs. Inglethorp's bell wire was neatly cut, and on Monday evening Mademoiselle Cynthia was spending the night with friends, so that Mrs. Inglethorp would have been quite alone in the right wing, completely shut off from help of any kind, and would have died, in all probability, before medical aid could have been summoned. But in her hurry to be in time for the village entertainment Mrs. Inglethorp forgot to take her medicine, and the next day she lunched away from home, so that the last—and fatal—dose was actually taken twenty-four hours later than had been anticipated by the murderer; and it is owing to that delay that the final proof—the last link of the chain—is now in my hands.
context: Poirot reconstructs the murderers' original schedule and isolation plan.

text: A letter in the murderer's own hand-writing, *mes amis!* Had it been a little clearer in its terms, it is possible that Mrs. Inglethorp, warned in time, would have escaped.
context: Poirot presents the final documentary proof against the murderer.

text: I did not deceive you, *mon ami*. At most, I permitted you to deceive yourself.
context: Poirot answers Hastings's complaint that he was deliberately misled.

text: You see, my friend, you have a nature so honest, and a countenance so transparent, that—*enfin*, to conceal your feelings is impossible!
context: Poirot explains why Hastings could not be trusted with the true theory.

text: This attitude of his gave me furiously to think, and I was slowly forced to the conclusion that Alfred Inglethorp wanted to be arrested. *Eh bien!* from that moment, I was equally determined that he should not be arrested.
context: Poirot explains his strategic response to Alfred's plan.

text: Because, *mon ami*, it is the law of your country that a man once acquitted can never be tried again for the same offence. Aha! but it was clever—his idea!
context: Poirot reveals Alfred's plan to secure permanent protection through acquittal.

text: And, then, there was that rather over-vehement hatred of hers! It concealed a very opposite emotion. There was, undoubtedly, a tie of passion between them long before he came to Styles.
context: Poirot interprets Miss Howard's hatred as concealment of passion for Alfred.

text: Never mind. Console yourself, my friend. We may hunt together again, who knows?
context: Poirot consoles Hastings over his romantic disappointments and suggests future cases together.

text: You have slept well, yes? You have recovered from the crossing so terrible? It is a marvel, almost you are exact this morning. _Pardon___, but your tie is not symmetrical. Permit that I rearrange him.
context: Poirot greets Hastings at breakfast and corrects his tie.

text: The true work, it is done from _within___. _The little grey cells___—remember always the little grey cells, _mon ami!___
context: Hastings recalls Poirot's maxim about detective work.

text: _Du tout!___ If for an hour one experiences sensations and emotions of the most terrible, one has lived many hours! Does not one of your English poets say that time is counted, not by hours, but by heart-beats?
context: Poirot defends his dramatic view of seasickness to Hastings.

text: Because he was an Englishman, an Islander to whom _la Manche___ was nothing. Oh, you English! With _nous autres___ it is different. Figure to yourself that a lady of my acquaintance at the beginning of the war fled to Ostend. There she had a terrible crisis of the nerves.
context: Poirot contrasts English attitudes to the Channel with continental ones.

text: Impossible to escape further except by crossing the sea! And she had a horror—_mais une horreur!___—of the sea! What was she to do? Daily _les Boches___ were drawing nearer. Imagine to yourself the terrible situation!
context: Poirot continues an anecdote about a woman terrified of crossing the sea.

text: Fortunately her husband was _homme pratique___. He was also very calm, the crises of the nerves, they affected him not. _Il l'a emportée simplement!___ Naturally when she reached England she was prostrate, but she still breathed.
context: Poirot finishes the seasickness anecdote.

text: This piece of toast. You remark him not?
context: Poirot draws Hastings's attention to an irregular piece of toast.

text: Is it square? No. Is it a triangle? Again no. Is it even round?
context: Poirot interrogates the shape of the toast.

text: No. Is it of any shape remotely pleasing to the eye? What symmetry have we here? None.
context: Poirot concludes that the toast has no pleasing symmetry.

text: What an intelligence has my friend Hastings!
context: Poirot sarcastically reacts to Hastings's explanation of the toast.

text: Comprehend you not that I have forbidden such a loaf—a loaf haphazard and shapeless, that no baker should permit himself to bake!
context: Poirot complains about the shapeless loaf.

text: I have not yet examined my letters, but nothing of interest arrives nowadays. The great criminals, the criminals of method, they do not exist. The cases I have been employed upon lately were _banal___ to the last degree. In verity I am reduced to recovering lost lap-dogs for fashionable ladies! The last problem that presented any interest was that intricate little affair of the Yardly diamond, and that was—how many months ago, my friend?
context: Poirot laments the lack of interesting criminal cases.

text: He merely thanks me (in his fashion) for a little point in the Aberystwyth Case on which I was able to set him right. I am delighted to have been of service to him.
context: Poirot describes helping Inspector Japp.

text: He is kind enough to say that I am a wonderful sport for my age, and that he was glad to have had the chance of letting me in on the case.
context: Poirot reports Japp's backhanded thanks.

text: This is out of the ordinary, _mon ami___. Read for yourself.
context: Poirot recognizes that Renauld's letter is unusual.

text: See you, my friend, there is no time to lose. The Continental express leaves Victoria at 11 o'clock. Do not agitate yourself. There is plenty of time. We can allow ten minutes for discussion.
context: Poirot organizes the immediate departure for France.

text: You accompany me, _n'est-ce pas?___
context: Poirot assumes Hastings will accompany him.

text: Ta-ta-ta. I will manage M. Renauld. By the way, I seem to know the name?
context: Poirot dismisses Hastings's concern about client privacy.

text: But without doubt. That explains the mention of Santiago. Santiago is in Chile, and Chile it is in South America! Ah, but we progress finely.
context: Poirot identifies the geographical significance of Santiago.

text: Do not be too sure of that, my friend. A rich man and his money are not so easily parted. Me, I have seen a well-known millionaire turn out a tramful of people to seek for a dropped halfpenny.
context: Poirot warns Hastings not to expect easy money from a millionaire.

text: it is not the money which attracts me here. Certainly it will be pleasant to have _carte blanche___ in our investigations; one can be sure that way of wasting no time, but it is something a little bizarre in this problem which arouses my interest. You remarked the postscript? How did it strike you?
context: Poirot explains what interests him in Renauld's appeal.

text: You are in error. See you not that while the ink of the signature is nearly black, that of the postscript is quite pale?
context: Poirot points out the different ink tones in the letter and postscript.

text: _Mon Dieu, mon ami___, but use your little grey cells! Is it not obvious? M. Renauld wrote his letter. Without blotting it, he reread it carefully. Then, not on impulse, but deliberately, he added those last words, and blotted the sheet.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Renauld added the postscript.

text: _Parbleu!___ so that it should produce the effect upon me that it has upon you.
context: Poirot explains the intended psychological effect of the postscript.

text: _Mais, oui___—to make sure of my coming! He reread the letter and was dissatisfied. It was not strong enough!
context: Poirot says Renauld strengthened the letter to ensure his arrival.

text: And so, _mon ami___, since that postscript was added, not on impulse, but soberly, in cold blood, the urgency is very great, and we must reach him as soon as possible.
context: Poirot concludes that the deliberately added postscript proves great urgency.

text: Ah! _je m'en fiche!___ Her case was certainly not interesting.
context: Poirot dismisses the Countess's case.

text: Because in that case she would have come, not written. A woman cannot wait—always remember that, Hastings.
context: Poirot generalizes about a woman with an urgent case.

text: Is it that you have forgotten the method most excellent of Laverguier? His system, I practise it always. One balances oneself, if you remember, turning the head from left to right, breathing in and out, counting six between each breath.
context: Poirot explains his method for preventing seasickness.

text: _Quelle idée!___ You do not figure to yourself that I shall go to Santiago?
context: Poirot rejects the idea of traveling to Santiago.

text: But certainly. I have never said otherwise. The trained observer, the expert, without doubt he is useful! But the others, the Hercules Poirots, they are above the experts! To them the experts bring the facts, their business is the method of the crime, its logical deduction, the proper sequence and order of the facts; above all, the true psychology of the case.
context: Poirot distinguishes expert observation from his own higher-order reasoning.

text: You did not descend from your horse and run along the ground smelling with your nose and uttering loud Ow Ows?
context: Poirot ridicules the idea that a detective should imitate a hound.

text: So. You leave the work of the d— hounds to the hounds. Yet you demand that I, Hercule Poirot, should make myself ridiculous by lying down (possibly on damp grass) to study hypothetical footprints, and should scoop up cigarette ash when I do not know one kind from the other. Remember the Plymouth Express mystery. The good Japp departed to make a survey of the railway line.
context: Poirot explains why he delegates physical clue-gathering.

text: When he returned, I, without having moved from my apartments, was able to tell him exactly what he had found.
context: Poirot boasts that he predicted Japp's findings without leaving home.

text: Not at all, since his evidence confirmed my theory. But _I___ should have wasted my time if _I___ had gone. It is the same with so called 'experts.' Remember the handwriting testimony in the Cavendish Case. One counsel's questioning brings out testimony as to the resemblances, the defence brings evidence to show dissimilarity. All the language is very technical.
context: Poirot explains why Japp's fieldwork confirmed rather than replaced his theory.

text: And the result? What we all knew in the first place. The writing was very like that of John Cavendish. And the psychological mind is faced with the question 'Why?' Because it was actually his? Or because some one wished us to think it was his?
context: Poirot analyzes the limits of expert handwriting evidence.

text: I answered that question, _mon ami___, and answered it correctly.
context: Poirot asserts that he answered the psychological question correctly.

text: For you, yes. For me, I have work to do, remember, at our journey's end.
context: Poirot reminds Hastings that the journey is work, not a holiday.

text: You are what the Scotch people call 'fey,' Hastings. It presages disaster.
context: Poirot interprets Hastings's optimism as a bad omen.

text: I do not know. But I have a premonition—a _je ne sais quoi!___
context: Poirot admits to an undefined premonition.

text: that this is going to be a big affair—a long, troublesome problem that will not be easy to work out.
context: Poirot predicts a long and difficult case.

text: Already you have seen a goddess!
context: Poirot teases Hastings for immediately noticing a beautiful woman.

text: What is that you say? Murdered? When? How?
context: Poirot reacts to the news that Renauld has been murdered.

text: The Commissary of Police, he is without doubt within?
context: Poirot asks to contact the police commissary.

text: _Voilà!___ Will you have the goodness to see that this card is sent in to the commissary at once?
context: Poirot politely sends his card to the commissary.

text: M. Bex! This is indeed a pleasure.
context: Poirot greets an old police acquaintance and introduces Hastings.

text: So I have. I run a private business in London.
context: Poirot tells Bex that he now runs a private detective business.

text: Possibly you know it already. You were aware that I had been sent for?
context: Poirot asks whether the police knew Renauld had summoned him.

text: The dead man. It seems he knew an attempt was going to be made on his life. Unfortunately he sent for me too late.
context: Poirot explains that the victim foresaw danger but called too late.

text: When was the crime committed?
context: Poirot asks when the murder occurred.

text: M. le juge, I propose to remain. I did not arrive in time to prevent my client's death, but I feel myself bound in honour to discover the assassin.
context: Poirot declares an obligation to identify his murdered client's assassin.

text: I thank you, monsieur. You will comprehend that at present I am completely in the dark. I know nothing whatever.
context: Poirot candidly states that he begins with no knowledge of the case.

text: Pardon, monsieur, for interrupting, but was that a common practice of his?
context: Poirot checks whether Renauld commonly took early walks.

text: And he had been dead—how long?
context: Poirot asks how long Renauld had been dead.

text: H'm, that fixes it at between midnight and 3 a.m.
context: Poirot calculates the initial time window for death.

text: And the inmates of the house, monsieur?
context: Poirot asks who else was in the household.

text: Pardon, M. Bex, but without doubt M. Renauld could drive the car himself?
context: Poirot asks whether Renauld could drive his own car.

text: See you not? In his letter M. Renauld speaks of sending the car for me to Calais.
context: Poirot connects the absent chauffeur to Renauld's promise to send a car.

text: Doubtless that is so. But why hire a car when you have one of your own. Why choose yesterday to send away the chauffeur on a holiday—suddenly, at a moment's notice? Was it that for some reason he wanted him out of the way before we arrived?
context: Poirot questions why the chauffeur was suddenly sent away before his arrival.

text: And the assumption is, M. le juge—?
context: Poirot asks the magistrate to state the assumption behind Bella's letter.

text: The stab in the back, yes—but not the grave! That was laborious work, hard work—no woman dug that grave, monsieur. That was a man's doing.
context: Poirot argues that the grave implies male physical labor.

text: Hardly. A man like M. Renauld, who has led an adventurous life in out-of-the-way places, would not be likely to ask for protection against a woman.
context: Poirot doubts that an adventurous man would seek protection from a woman.

text: So. A legacy of a thousand pounds to Mr. Stonor—who is he, by the way?
context: Poirot notices a legacy in Renauld's will and asks about Stonor.

text: And everything else left unconditionally to his beloved wife, Eloise. Simply drawn up, but perfectly legal. Witnessed by the two servants, Denise and Françoise. Nothing so very unusual about that.
context: Poirot summarizes the will's disposition.

text: Man is a vain animal. M. Renauld figured to himself, without doubt, that his widow would never marry again. As to the son, it may have been a wise precaution to leave the money in his mother's hands. The sons of rich men are proverbially wild.
context: Poirot comments on vanity and the possible reason for leaving money to the wife.

text: Not a particle, _mon ami!___ And for once, perhaps, it is a pity.
context: Poirot approves the study's cleanliness while noting its investigative disadvantage.

text: You recognize it—eh, Hastings?
context: Poirot asks Hastings to recognize a paper fragment.

text: The moral of that is, always look under the mats! My friend Hastings here will tell you that anything in the least crooked is a torment to me. As soon as I saw that the hearth-rug was out of the straight, I said to myself: '_Tiens!___ The leg of the chair caught it in being pushed back. Possibly there may be something beneath it which the good Françoise overlooked.'
context: Poirot explains how the crooked rug led him to the hidden cheque fragment.

text: Or Denise, or Léonie. Whoever did this room. Since there is no dust, the room _must___ have been done this morning. I reconstruct the incident like this. Yesterday, possibly last night, M. Renauld drew a cheque to the order of some one named Duveen.
context: Poirot reconstructs the torn cheque incident from the cleaned room.

text: It was here that M. Renauld received his guest last night, eh?
context: Poirot tests whether the study was where Renauld received his visitor.

text: By _this___. I found it on the back of the leather chair.
context: Poirot reveals the woman's hair found on the chair.

text: _Ma foi!___ but it is sharp! A nice easy little tool for murder!
context: Poirot comments on the dagger's sharpness and suitability for murder.

text: No wonder the servants heard M. Renauld mounting the stairs; not a board of them but creaks fit to wake the dead!
context: Poirot observes that the creaking stairs should have exposed the alleged abduction.

text: The glass is broken, yes, but the watch itself is still going.
context: Poirot discovers that the smashed wrist watch is still running.

text: What times does the last train leave the station of Merlinville?
context: Poirot asks for the time of the last train from Merlinville.

text: _Pardon___, madame, but may I examine your wrists.
context: Poirot politely asks to examine Madame Renauld's wrists.

text: I am an imbecile, my friend! If ever there was love and grief in a woman's voice, I heard it then. My little idea was all wrong. _Eh bien!___ I must start again!
context: Poirot admits that Madame Renauld's genuine grief disproved his initial idea.

text: It is to me incredible that the servants heard nothing. The creaking of that staircase, with _three___ people descending it, would awaken the dead!
context: Poirot doubts that three people could descend the creaking stairs unheard.

text: What moved them in the first place to try if the front door were open? It was a most unlikely thing that it should be. It was far more probable that they should at once try to force a window.
context: Poirot questions why intruders would first test the front door.

text: That is the window of the bedroom we have just come from, is it not? And see—there is a tree by which it would be the easiest thing in the world to mount.
context: Poirot identifies a tree as an easy route to the bedroom window.

text: I do not agree with you. I have a little idea that these footprints are the most important things we have seen yet.
context: Poirot declares the apparently irrelevant footprints important.

text: And does this, too, belong to the murdered man?
context: Poirot asks whether a piece of lead piping also belongs to the victim.

text: M. Bex, tell me, I pray you, the meaning of this whitewashed line that extends all round the grave. Is it a device of the police?
context: Poirot asks about the white line surrounding the grave.

text: I? Never! What a game!
context: Poirot criticizes golf for its lack of mathematical symmetry.

text: It was not a very good choice they made—of a spot to bury the body? When the men began to dig up the ground, all would have been discovered.
context: Poirot notes that planned golf work would expose the buried body.

text: At last you have seen the detective you admire—the human foxhound! Is it not so, my friend?
context: Poirot teases Hastings about admiring Giraud's physical methods.

text: _Eh bien!___ I also have found something! A piece of lead-piping.
context: Poirot counters that he too found a clue: the lead piping.

text: Well, where is this 'secret'? Presumably some distance away, since they wish him to dress himself. Yet he is found murdered close at hand, almost within ear-shot of the house. Then again, it is pure chance that a weapon such as the dagger should be lying about casually, ready to hand.
context: Poirot questions the location of the alleged secret and the convenient dagger.

text: My friend, I am about to surprise you—to please you! I have taken your reproaches to heart! We will examine some footprints!
context: Poirot announces that he will examine footprints to please Hastings.

text: In that right-hand bed yonder. M. Bex says that they are the footmarks of the gardener. Let us see if that is so. See, he approaches with his wheelbarrow.
context: Poirot identifies which flower-bed he intends to test.

text: He will not think about it at all.
context: Poirot predicts the gardener will not think about his request.

text: I was admiring these magnificent geraniums. They are truly superb. They have been planted long?
context: Poirot flatters the gardener about the geraniums.

text: You put in some new plants yesterday, didn't you? Those in the middle there, and in the other bed also?
context: Poirot uses the plants to confirm when the beds were replanted.

text: That the foot would be inside the boot? You do not use your excellent mental capacities sufficiently. Well, what of the footmark?
context: Poirot chides Hastings and asks him to assess the footprint.

text: _Mon Dieu!___ But what an idiom! What does it mean?
context: Poirot reacts to Hastings's English idiom.

text: No, no, _mon ami___. At last I am on the right track. I am still in the dark, but, as I hinted just now to M. Bex, these footmarks are the most important and interesting things in the case! That poor Giraud—I should not be surprised if he took no notice of them whatever.
context: Poirot insists the footprints remain the most important clue.

text: The secrecy, it is impossible in an affair of this kind! Not that it matters. Well, mademoiselle, what is it you want to know?
context: Poirot observes that secrecy is impossible and invites Marthe's question.

text: Suspicion is in the air at present, mademoiselle.
context: Poirot evasively tells Marthe that suspicion is in the air.

text: Why do you want to know?
context: Poirot directly asks why Marthe wants to know who is suspected.

text: Their names are unknown, but they are presumed to be Chilians from Santiago. And now, mademoiselle, you see what comes of being young and beautiful! I have betrayed professional secrets for you!
context: Poirot gives Marthe a misleading answer and playfully blames her beauty.

text: _Mon Dieu!___ But it is that you have the susceptible heart!
context: Poirot comments on Hastings's susceptible heart.

text: When you were in love with two charming women at once, and neither of them were for you? Yes, I remember.
context: Poirot recalls Hastings's earlier romantic failures.

text: Ah, _mon ami___, do not set your heart on Marthe Daubreuil. She is not for you, that one! Take it from Papa Poirot!
context: Poirot warns Hastings not to fall in love with Marthe.

text: Ta-ta-ta! Do not excite yourself! I have not said that I suspected her. But you must admit that her anxiety to know about the case is somewhat unusual.
context: Poirot denies accusing Marthe while noting her unusual anxiety.

text: I may be mistaken, but I rather fancy that it was a murder case!
context: Poirot tentatively identifies the old case as a murder case.

text: What you say is very just, but what will you? The heart of a woman who loves will forgive many blows. Still, undoubtedly there must have been many scenes of recrimination between them in the last few months?
context: Poirot reflects on a loving woman's capacity to forgive.

text: No, I shall await their return in the _salon___. It is cool there on this hot morning.
context: Poirot chooses to wait calmly in the salon.

text: The one he _did___ light his cigarette with. You have found that also?
context: Poirot presses Giraud on whether he found the used match.

text: Perhaps you didn't search very thoroughly.
context: Poirot needles Giraud about the thoroughness of his search.

text: Ah, that, unfortunately, I cannot for the moment remember—but I shall do so. I had hoped you might be able to assist me.
context: Poirot admits he cannot yet recall the earlier crime but expects to.

text: There is such a thing as the individual touch.
context: Poirot introduces the idea of an individual psychological signature in crime.

text: I am speaking to you now of the psychology of crime. M. Giraud knows quite well that each criminal has his particular method, and that the police, when called in to investigate—say a case of burglary—can often make a shrewd guess at the offender, simply by the peculiar method he has employed. (Japp would tell you the same, Hastings.) Man is an unoriginal animal. Unoriginal within the law in his daily respectable life, equally unoriginal outside the law. If a man commits a crime, any other crime he commits will resemble it closely. The English murderer who disposed of his wives in succession by drowning them in their baths was a case in point.
context: Poirot lectures that criminals repeat characteristic methods.

text: Had he varied his methods, he might have escaped detection to this day. But he obeyed the common dictates of human nature, arguing that what had once succeeded would succeed again, and he paid the penalty of his lack of originality.
context: Poirot explains how repetition led a serial wife-murderer to detection.

text: That when you have two crimes precisely similar in design and execution, you find the same brain behind them both. I am looking for that brain, M. Giraud—and I shall find it. Here we have a true clue—a psychological clue. You may know all about cigarettes and match ends, M. Giraud, but I, Hercule Poirot, know the mind of man!
context: Poirot declares that identical crimes reveal the same mind and contrasts himself with Giraud.

text: You are quite right, Hastings. From the beginning I have been sure that she was keeping something back. At first I suspected her, if not of inspiring, at least of conniving at the crime.
context: Poirot agrees that Madame Renauld is concealing something.

text: But certainly! She benefits enormously—in fact, by this new will, she is the only person to benefit. So, from the start, she was singled out for attention. You may have noticed that I took an early opportunity of examining her wrists. I wished to see whether there was any possibility that she had gagged and bound herself.
context: Poirot explains why Madame Renauld was an initial suspect.

text: _Eh bien___, I saw at once that there was no fake, the cords had actually been drawn so tight as to cut into the flesh. That ruled out the possibility of her having committed the crime single-handed. But it was still possible for her to have connived at it, or to have been the instigator with an accomplice. Moreover, the story, as she told it, was singularly familiar to me—the masked men that she could not recognize, the mention of 'the secret'—I had heard, or read, all these things before. Another little detail confirmed my belief that she was not speaking the truth.
context: Poirot uses the wrist marks and familiar story to refine his suspicion.

text: You see, _mon ami?___ You comprehend?
context: Poirot checks whether Hastings follows his reasoning.

text: _C'est bien!___ Let us then employ our little grey cells. Tell me, my friend, at what time, according to you, did the tragedy take place?
context: Poirot directs Hastings to use the little grey cells and fix the time of death.

text: Exactly, and on the strength of that, you, the examining magistrate, Bex, and every one else, accept the time without further question. But I, Hercule Poirot, say that Madame Renauld lied. _The crime took place at least two hours earlier.___
context: Poirot rejects the accepted timeline and declares the crime earlier.

text: They declared, after examination of the body, that death had taken place between ten and seven hours previously. _Mon ami___, for some reason, it was imperative that the crime should seem to have taken place later than it actually did. You have read of a smashed watch or clock recording the exact hour of a crime? So that the time should not rest on Mrs. Renauld's testimony alone, some one moved on the hands of that wrist watch to two o'clock, and then dashed it violently to the ground. But, as is often the case, they defeated their own object.
context: Poirot explains why the apparent time of the crime was deliberately postponed.

text: The glass was smashed, but the mechanism of the watch was uninjured. It was a most disastrous manoeuvre on their part, for it at once drew my attention to two points—first, that Madame Renauld was lying: secondly, that there must be some vital reason for the postponement of the time.
context: Poirot explains how the running watch exposed the deception.

text: Ah, that is the question! There we have the whole mystery. As yet, I cannot explain it. There is only one idea that presents itself to me as having a possible connection.
context: Poirot identifies the false time as the central mystery.

text: The last train left Merlinville at seventeen minutes past twelve.
context: Poirot supplies the last train's departure time.

text: Perfect, Hastings! You have it!
context: Poirot praises Hastings for recognizing the alibi implication.

text: Go by all means if you wish, _mon ami___—but if you go, I should not ask for particulars of two foreigners.
context: Poirot advises Hastings not to ask about two foreign men.

text: Là, là, you do not believe all that rigmarole, do you? The masked men and all the rest of _cette histoire-là!___
context: Poirot dismisses the masked-men story as rigmarole.

text: Undoubtedly there was a secret in M. Renauld's life—there can be no doubt of that. On the other hand, the word Santiago, to my mind, is a red herring, dragged continually across the track to put us off the scent. It is possible that it was used in the same way on M. Renauld, to keep him from directing his suspicions into a quarter nearer at hand. Oh, be assured, Hastings, the danger that threatened him was not in Santiago, it was near at hand, in France.
context: Poirot identifies Santiago as a red herring and locates the danger in France.

text: Planted! Deliberately planted there for Giraud or one of his tribe to find! Ah, he is smart, Giraud, he can do his tricks! So can a good retriever dog. He comes in so pleased with himself.
context: Poirot declares the match and cigarette end planted for a clue-hunter.

text: For hours he has crawled on his stomach. 'See what I have found,' he says. And then again to me: 'What do you see here?' Me, I answer, with profound and deep truth, 'Nothing.' And Giraud, the great Giraud, he laughs, he thinks to himself, 'Oh, that he is imbecile, this old one!' _But we shall see.…___
context: Poirot mocks Giraud's pride in finding planted evidence.

text: One person could tell us—Madame Renauld. But she will not speak. Threats and entreaties would not move her. A remarkable woman that, Hastings. I recognized as soon as I saw her that I had to deal with a woman of unusual character.
context: Poirot says only Madame Renauld can explain events and assesses her character.

text: At first, as I told you, I was inclined to suspect her of being concerned in the crime. Afterwards I altered my opinion.
context: Poirot says only Madame Renauld can explain events and assesses her character.

text: Her spontaneous and genuine grief at the sight of her husband's body. I could swear that the agony in that cry of hers was genuine.
context: Poirot cites Madame Renauld's spontaneous grief as evidence.

text: I beg your pardon, my friend—one can always be mistaken. Regard a great actress, does not her acting of grief carry you away and impress you with its reality? No, however strong my own impression and belief, I needed other evidence before I allowed myself to be satisfied. The great criminal can be a great actor. I base my certainty in this case, not upon my own impression, but upon the undeniable fact that Mrs. Renauld actually fainted.
context: Poirot warns that apparent emotion can be acted and demands evidence.

text: I turned up her eyelids and felt her pulse. There was no deception—the swoon was genuine. Therefore I was satisfied that her anguish was real and not assumed. Besides, a small additional point not without interest, it was unnecessary for Mrs. Renauld to exhibit unrestrained grief. She had had one paroxysm on learning of her husband's death, and there would be no need for her to simulate another such a violent one on beholding his body.
context: Poirot explains how he verified that Madame Renauld truly fainted.

text: No, Mrs. Renauld was not her husband's murderess. But why has she lied? She lied about the wrist watch, she lied about the masked men—she lied about a third thing. Tell me, Hastings, what is your explanation of the open door?
context: Poirot concludes that Madame Renauld was not the murderess but had lied.

text: That is the explanation of Giraud. It does not satisfy me. There is a meaning behind that open door which for a moment I cannot fathom.
context: Poirot rejects Giraud's explanation of the open door.

text: _A la bonne heure!___ Let us hear it.
context: Poirot encourages Hastings to present a theory.

text: An admirable theory, Hastings, but for two facts which you have characteristically overlooked. In the first place, who gagged and bound Madame Renauld? And why on earth should they return to the house to do so? In the second place, no man on earth would go out to keep an appointment wearing his underclothes and an overcoat. There are circumstances in which a man might wear pajamas and an overcoat—but the other, never!
context: Poirot dismantles Hastings's appointment theory with two overlooked facts.

text: No—_and there ought to have been.___ Listen, Hastings. The gardener, Auguste, as you heard him say, planted both those beds the preceding afternoon. In the one there are plentiful impressions of his big hobnailed boots—in the other, _none!___ You see? Some one had passed that way, some one who, to obliterate their footprints, smoothed over the surface of the bed with a rake.
context: Poirot explains how the absence of footprints proves the flower-bed was raked.

text: That is possible of course. Yet I have a strong idea that they left by the window.
context: Poirot admits an alternative but maintains his intuition about the window.

text: Method, you comprehend! Method! Arrange your facts. Arrange your ideas. And if some little fact will not fit in—do not reject it but consider it closely.
context: Poirot states his rule for arranging facts and respecting anomalies.

text: Though its significance escapes you, be sure that it is significant.
context: Poirot states his rule for arranging facts and respecting anomalies.

text: But yes, it is better so. Before, there was at all events a clear theory as to how and by whose hands he met his death. Now that is all gone. We are in darkness. A hundred conflicting points confuse and worry us.
context: Poirot welcomes confusion after the collapse of a simple theory.

text: That is well. That is excellent. Out of confusion comes forth order. But if you find order to start with, if a crime seems simple and above-board, _eh bien, méfiez vous!___ It is, how do you say it?—_cooked!___ The great criminal is simple—but very few criminals _are___ great.
context: Poirot explains that suspiciously tidy crimes are manufactured.

text: In trying to cover up their tracks, they invariably betray themselves. Ah, _mon ami___, I would that some day I could meet a really great criminal—one who commits his crime, and then—does nothing! Even I, Hercule Poirot, might fail to catch such a one.
context: Poirot says criminals betray themselves by covering tracks and imagines a truly great criminal.

text: It may be—yes, it may be. But as yet I do not know! There is something very deep underneath all this. You will see. Something very deep.
context: Poirot says a deep hidden matter remains unresolved.

text: _Eh bien!___ And your indiscretions! You recount them not?
context: Poirot playfully asks Hastings to recount his indiscretions.

text: _Tiens!___ A story of the most romantic. What is her name, this charming young lady?
context: Poirot calls Hastings's encounter romantic and asks the woman's name.

text: Still more romantic! The first _rencontre___ in the train from Paris, the second here. Journeys end in lovers' meetings, is not that the saying?
context: Poirot romanticizes Hastings's repeated encounters with Cinderella.

text: Yesterday it was Mademoiselle Daubreuil, today it is Mademoiselle—Cinderella! Decidedly you have the heart of a Turk, Hastings! You should establish a harem!
context: Poirot jokes that Hastings should establish a harem.

text: You believe, then, in birth and breeding?
context: Poirot probes Hastings's belief in birth and breeding.

text: I agree with you, _mon ami___. Ninety-nine times out of a hundred, it is as you say. But there is always the hundredth time! Still, that does not arise, as you do not propose to see the lady again.
context: Poirot allows for rare exceptions to class-based marriage rules.

text: Ah, my friend, have faith in Papa Poirot. Some day, if you permit, I will arrange you a marriage of great suitability.
context: Poirot offers to arrange a suitable marriage for Hastings.

text: Mademoiselle Cinderella is staying at the Hôtel d'Angleterre you told me, did you not?
context: Poirot tests the name of Cinderella's hotel.

text: To look for the murderer of M. Renauld.
context: Poirot says he is going to Paris to look for the murderer.

text: I am quite certain that he is not. Nevertheless, it is there that I must look for him. You do not understand, but I will explain it all to you in good time. Believe me, this journey to Paris is necessary. I shall not be away long.
context: Poirot explains that Paris is necessary despite the murderer not being there.

text: In all probability I shall return tomorrow. I do not propose that you should accompany me. Remain here and keep an eye on Giraud. Also cultivate the society of M. Renauld _fils___. And thirdly, if you wish, endeavour to cut him out with Mademoiselle Marthe.
context: Poirot assigns Hastings tasks and teases him about Marthe.

text: _Mon ami___—I know human nature. Throw together a boy young Renauld and a beautiful girl like Mademoiselle Marthe, and the result is almost inevitable. Then, the quarrel! It was money or a woman and, remembering Léonie's description of the lad's anger, I decided on the latter. So I made my guess—and I was right.
context: Poirot explains how human nature led him to infer Jack's love affair.

text: At any rate—_I saw that she had anxious eyes.___ That is how always think of Mademoiselle Daubreuil _as the girl with the anxious eyes.…___
context: Poirot names Marthe by the anxiety he observed in her eyes.

text: I fancy, my friend, that we shall see before very long. But I must start.
context: Poirot predicts that Marthe's anxiety will soon be explained.

text: Perhaps—perhaps. But I like plenty of leisure at the station. I do not wish to rush, to hurry, to excite myself.
context: Poirot explains his preference for ample time and no rushing.

text: You will do nothing of the sort. I forbid it.
context: Poirot firmly forbids Hastings from seeing him off.

text: I mean it, _mon ami___. Au revoir! You permit that I embrace you? Ah, no, I forget that it is not the English custom. Une poignee de main, alors.
context: Poirot adjusts his affectionate farewell to English custom.

text: _Mon cher ami___, I have succeeded—but succeeded to a marvel!
context: Poirot returns from Paris and announces success.

text: How would you that I should hear anything? There have been some developments, eh? The brave Giraud, he has made an arrest? Or even arrests perhaps? Ah, but I will make him look foolish, that one!
context: Poirot guesses at developments and boasts he will outdo Giraud.

text: But where are you taking me, my friend? Do we not go to the hotel? It is necessary that I attend to my moustaches—they are deplorably limp from the heat of travelling. Also, without doubt, there is dust on my coat. And my tie, that I must rearrange.
context: Poirot worries about his moustache, dust, and tie after travel.

text: What is that you say? Another murder? Ah, then, I am all wrong. I have failed. Giraud may mock himself at me—he will have reason!
context: Poirot reacts to the second murder by admitting his theory may have failed.

text: I? Not the least in the world. It demolishes my theory—it ruins everything—it—ah, no!
context: Poirot recovers from shock and insists methodical facts permit only one explanation.

text: The victim is a man of middle-age. His body was found in the locked shed near the scene of the crime and had been dead at least forty-eight hours. And it is most probable that he was stabbed in a similar manner to M. Renauld, though not necessarily in the back.
context: Poirot deduces the second victim's age, location, time of death, and wound before hearing details.

text: Would I do such a thing? I assure you that I have heard nothing whatsoever. Did you not observe the shock your news was to me?
context: Poirot denies having prior knowledge and points to his genuine shock.

text: I was right then? But I knew it. The little grey cells, my friend, the little grey cells! They told me. Thus, and in no other way, could there have been a second death.
context: Poirot credits the little grey cells and requests the full account.

text: Now tell me all. If we go round to the left here, we can take a short cut across the golf links which will bring us to the back of the Villa Geneviève much more quickly.
context: Poirot credits the little grey cells and requests the full account.

text: The dagger was in the wound, you say? That is curious. You are sure it was the same one?
context: Poirot focuses on whether the same dagger was used.

text: Nothing is impossible. There may have been two daggers.
context: Poirot proposes the existence of two identical daggers.

text: You speak as usual, without reflection, Hastings. In some cases two identical weapons _would___ be highly improbable. But not here. This particular weapon was a war souvenir which was made to Jack Renauld's orders. It is really highly unlikely, when you come to think of it, that he should have had only one made.
context: Poirot explains why two duplicate war-souvenir daggers are plausible.

text: Very probably he would have another for his own use.
context: Poirot explains why two duplicate war-souvenir daggers are plausible.

text: My friend, in working upon a case, one does not take into account only the things that are 'mentioned.' There is no reason to mention many things which may be important. Equally, there is often an excellent reason for _not___ mentioning them. You can take your choice of the two motives.
context: Poirot teaches that investigators must consider unmentioned facts and motives for silence.

text: There is foam on the lips. You observed it?
context: Poirot directs the doctor's attention to foam on the dead man's lips.

text: But you observe it now?
context: Poirot confirms that the doctor now observes the foam.

text: It is a strange wound, this! It has not bled. There is no stain on the clothes. The blade of the dagger is slightly discoloured, that is all. What do you think, M. le docteur?
context: Poirot identifies the bloodless wound as strange and consults the doctor.

text: It is not abnormal at all. It is most simple. The man was stabbed _after he was dead___.
context: Poirot concludes that the second body was stabbed after death.

text: M. Giraud agrees with me, do you not, monsieur?
context: Poirot challenges Giraud to agree with his conclusion.

text: I should fancy it was done quite cold-bloodedly—to create an impression.
context: Poirot says the post-mortem stabbing was intended to create an impression.

text: He was not killed. He died. He died, M. le juge, if I am not much mistaken, of an epileptic fit!
context: Poirot concludes that the man died naturally in an epileptic fit.

text: A woman's hair? What woman's, I wonder?
context: Poirot questions whose hair was wound around the dagger.

text: I wonder in what direction he hopes to mislead me? A woman's hair—h'm!
context: Poirot suspects Giraud is trying to mislead him with the hair.

text: Willingly, my friend. I went to Paris to find _this___.
context: Poirot says his Paris journey was to obtain a photograph.

text: You recognize it, my friend?
context: Poirot asks Hastings to recognize the photograph.

text: Not quite correct, my friend. She did not call herself by that name in those days. That is a picture of the notorious Madame Beroldy!
context: Poirot identifies Madame Daubreuil as the notorious Madame Beroldy.

text: I pass to another objection. You have spoken of the similarity between the two crimes. Wherein does that lie, my friend?
context: Poirot asks Hastings to define the similarity between the two cases.

text: Do not be so indignant, I beg of you. I repudiate nothing. The similarity of the two stories links the two cases together inevitably. But reflect now on something very curious. It is not Madame Daubreuil who tells us this tale—if it were all would indeed be plain sailing—it is Madame Renauld.
context: Poirot separates the shared story from the women who told it.

text: Is she then in league with the other?
context: Poirot separates the shared story from the women who told it.

text: _Mon ami___, you must make your own deductions. You have 'access to the facts!' Concentrate your grey cells. Reason—not like Giraud—but like Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot orders Hastings to reason from the facts like Hercule Poirot.

text: My friend, in many ways I have been an imbecile. But at last I see clearly.
context: Poirot admits past foolishness but says he now sees clearly.

text: I have discovered what M. Renauld sent for me to discover.
context: Poirot says he has discovered what Renauld summoned him to find.

text: We talk a little at cross-purposes. There are here not one crime, but two. The first I have solved, the second—_eh bien___, I will confess, I am not sure!
context: Poirot distinguishes two crimes and admits the second remains uncertain.

text: Still you do not understand. One may have a crime without a murderer, but for two crimes it is essential to have two bodies.
context: Poirot explains that two crimes require two bodies, not necessarily two murderers.

text: And without doubt we are not original in the idea, Hastings. The excellent Giraud, he also has probably made his inquiries.
context: Poirot assumes Giraud has also checked the railway station.

text: Take a seat, monsieur. I regret infinitely to derange you, but you will perhaps understand that the atmosphere of the Villa is not too congenial to me. M. Giraud and I do not see eye to eye about everything. His politeness to me has not been striking and you will comprehend that I do not intend any little discoveries I may make to benefit him in any way.
context: Poirot explains to Jack why he is withholding discoveries from Giraud.

text: Then I may ask a little favour of you?
context: Poirot politely asks Jack for a small favor.

text: I will ask you to go to the railway station and take a train to the next station along the line, Abbalac. Ask there at the cloak-room whether two foreigners deposited a valise there on the night of the murder. It is a small station, and they are almost certain to remember. Will you do this?
context: Poirot sends Jack on a fabricated railway inquiry.

text: One moment, M. Renauld, there is one little matter that puzzles me. Why did you not mention to M. Hautet this morning that you were in Merlinville on the night of the crime?
context: Poirot confronts Jack about concealing his presence in Merlinville.

text: Then it is a singular mistake that I have made there—for it is shared by the station staff. They say you arrived by the 11:40 train.
context: Poirot cites station staff against Jack's denial.

text: I should like an explanation of the reason that brought you here.
context: Poirot requests the reason for Jack's secret visit.

text: And you did see her?
context: Poirot presses Jack on whether he actually saw Marthe.

text: St. Beauvais? That is fifteen kilometres. A long walk, M. Renauld.
context: Poirot notes that Jack's claimed walk was unusually long.

text: Quick, Hastings. We will go after him.
context: Poirot urgently tells Hastings to follow Jack.

text: All is well. He has taken the bait. He will go to Abbalac, and will inquire for the mythical valise left by the mythical foreigners. Yes, _mon ami___, all that was a little invention of my own.
context: Poirot reveals that the valise errand was a deliberate invention.

text: Your penetration is amazing, Hastings! Now, if you please, we will go up to the Villa Geneviève.
context: Poirot sarcastically praises Hastings for seeing the obvious purpose.

text: And how did you know I had been there?
context: Poirot asks how Hastings learned of his hotel inquiry.

text: If I have offended your susceptibilities in any way, I demand pardon of you. You will understand better soon. But, believe me, I have striven to concentrate all my energies on the case.
context: Poirot apologizes for offending Hastings while defending his focus on the case.

text: A little word with you, mademoiselle, if it is permitted?
context: Poirot politely requests a private word with Marthe.

text: Mademoiselle, do you remember running after me on the road the day that I came to your house with the examining magistrate? You asked me if any one were suspected of the crime.
context: Poirot reminds Marthe of her earlier question about suspects.

text: Will you ask me the same question again, mademoiselle?
context: Poirot invites Marthe to repeat her earlier question.

text: This. If you were to ask me that question again, I should give you a different answer. Some one is suspected—but not a Chilian.
context: Poirot tells Marthe the suspect is no longer a Chilian.

text: You know, of course, that he was here on the night of the murder?
context: Poirot asks whether Marthe knew Jack was present on the murder night.

text: At any rate, tell us, mademoiselle.
context: Poirot invites Marthe to tell the apparently absurd story.

text: But why did you not say so at the time, mademoiselle?
context: Poirot asks why Marthe did not identify the tramp earlier.

text: Money of course. Remember that Jack Renauld thought that he would come in to half his father's fortune at the latter's death.
context: Poirot supplies money as Jack's possible motive.

text: Giraud would say that he was an accomplice—an apache who helped young Renauld to commit the crime, and who was conveniently put out of the way afterwards.
context: Poirot predicts how Giraud would fit the tramp into his theory.

text: That is the cream of Giraud's little jest. According to him, it is not a woman's hair at all. Remember that the youths of today wear their hair brushed straight back from the forehead with pomade or hairwash to make it lie flat. Consequently some of the hairs are of considerable length.
context: Poirot explains Giraud's claim that the long hair belonged to a young man.

text: I wish to make a search amongst the effects of M. Jack Renauld. That is why I had to get him out of the way for a few hours.
context: Poirot reveals that Jack's false errand enabled a search of his belongings.

text: Of course. He builds a case, as a beaver builds a dam, with a fatiguing industry. But he will not have looked for the things that I am seeking—in all probability he would not have seen their importance if they stared him in the face. Let us begin.
context: Poirot contrasts his search objectives with Giraud's industrious case-building.

text: What are your grounds for arresting him?
context: Poirot asks Giraud for the grounds of Jack's arrest.

text: As a matter of courtesy, yes.
context: Poirot invokes courtesy to obtain Giraud's reasoning.

text: You are wrong. There is one other person who could have taken the dagger.
context: Poirot says Giraud omitted another possible thief of the dagger.

text: Only that there is one thing you have failed to take into account.
context: Poirot says Giraud failed to account for one fact.

text: Jack Renauld was presumably acquainted with the planning out of the golf course. He knew that the body would be discovered almost at once, when they started to dig the bunker.
context: Poirot notes Jack knew the golf-course plan would expose the body.

text: I do not know, Hastings. There is just a chance of it. Of course Giraud is all wrong—wrong from beginning to end. If Jack Renauld is guilty, it is in spite of Giraud's arguments, not _because___ of them. And the gravest indictment against him is known only to me.
