context: Poirot says Giraud's case is wrong even if Jack proves guilty.

text: If you would use your grey cells, and see the whole case clearly as I do, you too would perceive it, my friend.
context: Poirot tells Hastings the gravest evidence is visible through proper use of the grey cells.

text: Let us walk this way to the sea. We will sit on that little mound there, overlooking the beach, and review the case. You shall know all that I know, but I would prefer that you should come at the truth by your own efforts—not by my leading you by the hand.
context: Poirot proposes a methodical review and wants Hastings to reach the truth himself.

text: You have a little idea, I see, _mon ami!___ Capital. We progress.
context: Poirot encourages Hastings's emerging idea.

text: _Enfin!___ You have arrived. And all by yourself. It is superb! Continue your reasoning. You are right.
context: Poirot enthusiastically praises Hastings for remembering Georges Conneau.

text: Decidedly we have done wrong to forget Georges Conneau.
context: Poirot enthusiastically praises Hastings for remembering Georges Conneau.

text: And the truth is necessarily correct. In your theory you made a fundamental error. You permitted your imagination to lead you astray with midnight assignations and passionate love scenes. But in investigating crime we must take our stand upon the commonplace. Shall I demonstrate my methods to you?
context: Poirot criticizes Hastings's romantic imagination and insists on the commonplace.

text: There was no mention of any name to show to whom the letter was addressed. We assumed it was to the dead man because it was in the pocket of his overcoat. Now, _mon ami___, something about that overcoat struck me as unusual. I measured it, and made the remark that he wore his overcoat very long. That remark should have given you to think.
context: Poirot explains how the overcoat showed the letter was misattributed.

text: We agree, _mon ami___—at least as to the cause. But what exact motive underlay this procedure of M. Renauld's?
context: Poirot asks for the motive behind Renauld's change of will.

text: Yet he wrote him affectionate letters to Paris?
context: Poirot tests the anger theory against Renauld's affectionate letters.

text: Now we come to the day of the tragedy. You have placed the events of the morning in a certain order. Have you any justification for that?
context: Poirot challenges Hastings's ordering of the morning's events.

text: I have ascertained that the letter to me was posted at the same time as the telegram was despatched. Masters was informed he could take a holiday shortly afterwards. In my opinion the quarrel with the tramp took place anterior to these happenings.
context: Poirot states the verified sequence of the letter, telegram, chauffeur, and tramp.

text: There is no need. I am sure of it. And if you do not see that, you see nothing, Hastings!
context: Poirot says no further questioning is needed because the sequence is clear.

text: You do not think it strange that he should use exactly the same expressions in his letter as Madame Renauld used later in her story? If the mention of Santiago was a blind, why should Renauld speak of it, and—what is more—send his son there?
context: Poirot compares Renauld's letter with Madame Renauld's later story.

text: My friend, my friend, where are your wits wandering? Remember the fragment of cheque, and the fact that the name Bella Duveen was faintly familiar to Stonor, and I think we may take it for granted that Bella Duveen is the full name of Jack's unknown correspondent, and that it was she who came to the Villa Geneviève that night. Whether she intended to see Jack, or whether she meant all along to appeal to his father we cannot be certain, but I think we may assume that this is what occurred. She produced her claim upon Jack, probably showed letters that he had written her, and the older man tried to buy her off by writing a cheque. This she indignantly tore up.
context: Poirot reconstructs Bella Duveen's visit using the cheque and Stonor's memory.

text: The terms of her letter are those of a woman genuinely in love, and she would probably deeply resent being offered money. In the end he got rid of her, and here the words that he used are significant.
context: Poirot infers Bella's resentment and the significance of Renauld's final words.

text: That is enough. He was desperately anxious for the girl to go. Why? Not only because the interview was unpleasant. No, it was the time that was slipping by, and for some reason time was precious.
context: Poirot infers that Renauld's urgency concerned slipping time.

text: That is what we ask ourselves. Why should it be? But later we have the incident of the wrist watch—which again shows us that time plays a very important part in the crime. We are now fast approaching the actual drama. It is half-past ten when Bella Duveen leaves, and by the evidence of the wrist watch we know that the crime was committed, or at any rate that it was staged, before twelve o'clock.
context: Poirot aligns Bella's departure, the wrist watch, and the staged crime.

text: We have reviewed all the events anterior to the murder, there remains only one unplaced. By the doctor's evidence, the tramp, when found, had been dead at least forty-eight hours—with a possible margin of twenty-four hours more. Now, with no other facts to help me than those we have discussed, I place the death as having occurred on the morning of June 7th.
context: Poirot places the tramp's death on the morning of June 7th.

text: Because only in that way can the sequence of events be logically explained. _Mon ami___, I have taken you step by step along the way. Do you not now see what is so glaringly plain?
context: Poirot says only one sequence logically explains all events.

text: That is just what I am not sure of as yet.
context: Poirot admits he is not yet sure who committed the actual murder.

text: We talk at cross-purposes, my friend. Remember, it is two crimes we are investigating—for which, as I pointed out to you, we have the necessary two bodies. There, there, _ne vous impatientez pas!___ I explain all. To begin with, we apply our psychology. We find three points at which M. Renauld displays a distinct change of view and action—three psychological points therefore.
context: Poirot separates the two crimes and begins a psychological analysis.

text: The first occurs immediately after arriving in Merlinville, the second after quarrelling with his son on a certain subject, the third on the morning of June 7th. Now for the three causes. We can attribute No. 1 to meeting Madame Daubreuil. No. 2 is indirectly connected with her since it concerns a marriage between M. Renauld's son and her daughter. But the cause of No. 3 is hidden from us.
context: Poirot identifies three changes in Renauld's behavior and their causes.

text: Exactly. Now Giraud laid it down as an axiom that a woman lies to save herself, the man she loves, and her child. Since we are satisfied that was Georges Conneau who dictated the lie to her, and as Georges Conneau is not Jack Renauld, follows that the third case is put out of court. And, still attributing the crime to Georges Conneau, the first is equally so. So we are forced to the second—that Madame Renauld lied for the sake of the man she loved—or in other words, for the sake of Georges Conneau.
context: Poirot applies Giraud's axiom about why women lie to identify Madame Renauld's motive.

text: _Bien!___ Madame Renauld loves Georges Conneau. Who, then, is Georges Conneau?
context: Poirot asks who Georges Conneau must be if Madame Renauld loved him.

text: Very well then. Do not cling to theories where facts no longer support them. Ask yourself instead who Madame Renauld _did___ love.
context: Poirot tells Hastings to abandon unsupported theories and ask whom Madame Renauld loved.

text: _Mais, oui___, you know perfectly. Who did Madame Renauld love so dearly that when she saw his dead body, she fell down in a swoon?
context: Poirot uses Madame Renauld's swoon to identify the man she loved.

text: Her husband—or Georges Conneau, whichever you like to call him.
context: Poirot reveals that Renauld and Georges Conneau are the same man.

text: that is just what he did do!
context: Poirot confirms that Georges Conneau planned his own apparent murder.

text: It seems strange to you, _mon ami___, that a man should plan his own death? So strange, that you prefer to reject the truth as fantastic, and to revert to a story that is in reality ten times more impossible. Yes, M. Renauld planned his own death, but there is one detail that perhaps escapes you—he did not intend to die.
context: Poirot explains that Renauld planned his death without intending to die.

text: For the crime that M. Renauld proposed a murderer was not necessary, as I told you, but a body was. Let us reconstruct, seeing events this time from a different angle.
context: Poirot explains that the staged crime required a body rather than a murderer.

text: Georges Conneau flies from justice—to Canada. There, under an assumed name he marries, and finally acquires a vast fortune in South America. But there is a nostalgia upon him for his own country. Twenty years have elapsed, he is considerably changed in appearance, besides being a man of such eminence that no one is likely to connect him with a fugitive from justice many years ago. He deems it quite safe to return.
context: Poirot reconstructs Georges Conneau's flight, new identity, fortune, and return.

text: He takes up his headquarters in England, but intends to spend the summers in France. And ill fortune, that obscure justice which shapes men's ends, and will not allow them to evade the consequences of their acts, takes him to Merlinville. There, in the whole of France, is the one person who is capable of recognizing him. It is, of course, a gold mine to Madame Daubreuil, and a gold mine of which she is not slow to take advantage. He is helpless, absolutely in her power.
context: Poirot explains how Madame Daubreuil recognized and blackmailed Renauld.

text: And she bleeds him heavily.
context: Poirot explains how Madame Daubreuil recognized and blackmailed Renauld.

text: And then the inevitable happens. Jack Renauld falls in love with the beautiful girl he sees almost daily, and wishes to marry her. That rouses his father. At all costs, he will prevent his son marrying the daughter of this evil woman. Jack Renauld knows nothing of his father's past, but Madame Renauld knows everything.
context: Poirot reconstructs Jack's love for Marthe and his father's opposition.

text: She is a woman of great force of character, and passionately devoted to her husband. They take counsel together. Renauld sees only one way of escape—death. He must appear to die, in reality escaping to another country where he will start again under an assumed name, and where Madame Renauld, having played the widow's part for a while, can join him. It is essential that she should have control of the money, so he alters his will.
context: Poirot explains how the Renaulds planned an apparent death and escape.

text: How they meant to manage the body business originally, I do not know—possibly an art student's skeleton—and a fire or something of the kind, but long before their plans have matured an event occurs which plays into their hands. A rough tramp, violent and abusive, finds his way into the garden. There is a struggle, M. Renauld seeks to eject him, and suddenly the tramp, an epileptic, falls down in a fit. He is dead. M. Renauld calls his wife. Together they drag him into the shed—as we know, the event had occurred just outside—and they realize the marvellous opportunity that has been vouchsafed them.
context: Poirot explains how the tramp's sudden death created the needed opportunity.

text: The man bears no resemblance to M. Renauld, but he is middle-aged, of a usual French type. That is sufficient.
context: Poirot explains why the tramp's general type was sufficient for the deception.

text: I rather fancy that they sat on the bench up there, out of earshot from the house, discussing matters. Their plan was quickly made. The identification must rest solely on Madame Renauld's evidence. Jack Renauld and the chauffeur (who had been with his master two years) must be got out of the way. It was unlikely that the French women servants would go near the body, and in any case Renauld intended to take measures to deceive any one not likely to appreciate details.
context: Poirot reconstructs the couple's planning and the need to remove witnesses.

text: Masters was sent off, a telegram despatched to Jack, Buenos Ayres being selected to give credence to the story that Renauld had decided upon. Having heard of me, as a rather obscure elderly detective, he wrote his appeal for help knowing that, when I arrived, the production of the letter would have a profound effect upon the examining magistrate—which, of course, it did.
context: Poirot explains the telegram, chauffeur's holiday, and calculated appeal to him.

text: They dressed the body of the tramp in a suit of M. Renauld's and left his ragged coat and trousers by the door of the shed, not daring to take them into the house. And then, to give credence to the tale Madame Renauld was to tell, they drove the aeroplane dagger through his heart. That night, M. Renauld will first bind and gag his wife, and then, taking a spade, will dig a grave in that particular spot of ground where he knows a—how do you call it? bunkair?—is to be made. It is essential that the body should be found—Madame Daubreuil must have no suspicions. On the other hand, if a little time elapses, any dangers as to identity will be greatly lessened.
context: Poirot reconstructs the disguise, post-mortem stabbing, binding, and grave.

text: Then, M. Renauld will don the tramp's rags, and shuffle off to the station, where he will leave, unnoticed, by the 12:10 train. Since the crime will be supposed to have taken place two hours later, no suspicion can possibly attach to him.
context: Poirot explains Renauld's intended train escape under a false time of death.

text: You see now his annoyance at the inopportune visit of the girl Bella. Every moment of delay is fatal to his plans. He gets rid of her as soon as he can, however. Then, to work! He leaves the front door slightly ajar to create the impression that the assassins left that way.
context: Poirot explains Bella's dangerous delay and the staged open door.

text: the justice that he has so long eluded overtakes him. An unknown hand stabs him in the back.… Now, Hastings, you understand what I mean when I talk of _two___ crimes. The first crime, the crime that M. Renauld, in his arrogance, asked us to investigate (ah, but he made a famous mistake there! He misjudged Hercule Poirot!) is solved. But behind it lies a deeper riddle.
context: Poirot reveals that an unknown hand killed Renauld during his own staging.

text: And to solve that will be difficult—since the criminal in his wisdom, has been content to avail himself of the devices prepared by M. Renauld. It has been a particularly perplexing and baffling mystery to solve. A young hand, like Giraud, who does not place any reliance on the psychology, is almost certain to fail.
context: Poirot reveals that an unknown hand killed Renauld during his own staging.

text: Ah, then you no longer despise poor old Papa Poirot? You shift your allegiance back from the human foxhound?
context: Poirot asks whether Hastings has returned his allegiance from Giraud.

text: Without doubt it is not all stupidity. He has had _la mauvaise chance___ once or twice. That dark hair coiled round the dagger, for instance. To say the least, it was misleading.
context: Poirot concedes that Giraud suffered misleading bad luck.

text: Madame Renauld's of course. That is where _la mauvaise chance___ came in. Her hair, dark originally, is almost completely silvered. It might just as easily have been a grey hair—and then, by no conceivable effort could Giraud have persuaded himself it came from the head of Jack Renauld! But it is all of a piece.
context: Poirot identifies the dark hair as Madame Renauld's and explains Giraud's error.

text: Always the facts must be twisted to fit the theory! Did not Giraud find the traces of two persons, a man and a woman, in the shed? And how does that fit in with his reconstruction of the case? I will tell you—it does not fit in, and so we shall hear no more of them! I ask you, is that a methodical way of working?
context: Poirot attacks Giraud for twisting facts to fit his theory.

text: The great Giraud! The great Giraud is nothing but a toy balloon—swollen with its own importance. But I, Hercule Poirot, whom he despises, will be the little pin that pricks the big balloon—_comme ça!___
context: Poirot compares Giraud to a swollen balloon and himself to the pin.

text: Without doubt, when Madame Renauld recovers, she will speak. The possibility of her son being accused of the murder never occurred to her. How should it, when she believed him safely at sea on board the _Anzora?___ _Ah! voilà une femme___, Hastings! What force, what self-command! She only made one slip.
context: Poirot explains why Madame Renauld never anticipated suspicion of her son.

text: On his unexpected return: 'It does not matter—_now___.' And no one noticed—no one realized the significance of those words. What a terrible part she has had to play, poor woman. Imagine the shock when she goes to identify the body and, instead of what she expects, sees the actual lifeless form of the husband she has believed miles away by now. No wonder she fainted! But since then, despite her grief and her despair, how resolutely she has played her part, and how the anguish of it must wring her.
context: Poirot admires Madame Renauld's self-command and interprets her one verbal slip.

text: She cannot say a word to set us on the track of the real murderers. For her son's sake, no one must know that Paul Renauld was Georges Conneau, the criminal. Final and most bitter blow, she has admitted publicly that Madame Daubreuil was her husband's mistress—for a hint of blackmail might be fatal to her secret. How cleverly she dealt with the examining magistrate when he asked her if there was any mystery in her husband's past life. 'Nothing so romantic, I am sure, M. le juge.' It was perfect, the indulgent tone, the _soupçon___ of sad mockery.
context: Poirot imagines the shock of identifying her actual husband's body.

text: At once M. Hautet felt himself foolish and melodramatic. Yes, she is a great woman! If she loved a criminal, she loved him royally!
context: Poirot explains why Madame Renauld maintained the lie despite grief.

text: You do not see? To disfigure the victim's face so that it would be unrecognizable. It was that which first set me on the right track. And that imbecile of a Giraud, swarming all over it to look for match ends! Did I not tell you that a clue of two feet long was quite as good as a clue of two inches?
context: Poirot explains that the lead piping was intended to disfigure the substitute body.

text: As I said before, will he? If he has arrived at the right person by the wrong method, he will not permit that to worry him.
context: Poirot predicts Giraud will not mind reaching the right person by the wrong method.

text: You see, Hastings, we must now start again. Who killed M. Renauld? Some one who was near the Villa just before twelve o'clock that night, some one who would benefit by his death—the description fits Jack Renauld only too well. The crime need not have been premeditated. And then the dagger!
context: Poirot starts the murder inquiry anew and lists the case against Jack.

text: Certainly, and, since they were duplicates, it stands to reason that Jack Renauld was the owner. But that would not trouble me so much. In fact I have a little idea as to that. No, the worst indictment against him is again psychological—heredity, _mon ami___, heredity! Like father, like son—Jack Renauld, when all is said or done, is the son of Georges Conneau.
context: Poirot says the strongest case against Jack is psychological heredity.

text: What time is the afternoon boat from Calais?
context: Poirot asks the time of the afternoon boat from Calais.

text: That will do very well. We shall just have time.
context: Poirot calculates that they have just enough time.

text: To find a possible—witness.
context: Poirot says he is going to find a possible witness.

text: I know nothing about her—but I can guess a good deal. We may take it for granted that her name _is___ Bella Duveen, and since that name was faintly familiar to M. Stonor, though evidently not in connection with the Renauld family, it is probable that she is on the stage. Jack Renauld was a young man with plenty of money, and twenty years of age. The stage is sure to have been the home of his first love. It tallies, too, with M. Renauld's attempt to placate her with a cheque.
context: Poirot deduces Bella's theatrical profession and how to find her.

text: I think I shall find her all right—especially with the help of _this___.
context: Poirot deduces Bella's theatrical profession and how to find her.

text: And you would swear to that in a court of law?
context: Poirot asks whether Hastings would swear to his alibi claim in court.

text: _Mon ami! Vive l'amour!___ It can perform miracles. It is decidedly ingenious what you have thought of there. It defeats even Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot mockingly praises Hastings's love-inspired false alibi.

text: So extraordinary that it gives one to think, does it not?
context: Poirot suggests an extraordinary fact deserves thought.

text: Good morning, M. Giraud. Come, Hastings.
context: Poirot dismisses Giraud and directs Hastings to leave.

text: And what are you doing here, M. Stonor?
context: Poirot asks Stonor why he is at the Villa.

text: Can you arrange for word to be sent to me at once should Madame Renauld recover consciousness?
context: Poirot asks to be notified immediately when Madame Renauld wakes.

text: if we are to work together, you and I, we must have things clear. First, I will ask you a question.
context: Poirot establishes clear terms before working with Bella.

text: Calm yourself, _petite___, I see that you know. Now a second question, did you know who M. Renauld was?
context: Poirot calms Bella and proceeds to a second question.

text: You did not give me a chance.
context: Poirot notes that Hastings gave him no chance.

text: Excite yourself not! Leave it to Papa Poirot. I can find her for you as soon as I have five little minutes!
context: Poirot reassures Hastings that he can find Dulcie quickly.

text: _Eh ma foi!___ and you were heading for it too!
context: Poirot tells Hastings he too was heading toward disaster.

text: You would have had Maître Grosíer's death from rage on your conscience if you had gone on.
context: Poirot jokes about the lawyer's likely rage.

text: Let us all go to Merlinville at once!
context: Poirot abruptly orders everyone back to Merlinville.

text: While you break it in person to Mademoiselle Marthe, eh?
context: Poirot teasingly identifies Jack's wish to tell Marthe personally.

text: But yes, by all means, I was about to propose such an arrangement myself.
context: Poirot agrees to Jack's proposed arrangement.

text: but women are very unexpected.
context: Poirot remarks that women are very unexpected.

text: But no, I tell you. It is better not.
context: Poirot advises Jack not to visit his mother.

text: In any case, do not take Mademoiselle with you. Mount, if you must, but you would be wise to be guided by me.
context: Poirot warns Jack not to take Marthe upstairs.

text: Where can we take him?
context: Poirot asks Marthe where Jack can be taken.

text: He has fever. The long strain begins to tell. And now this shock on top of it. Get him to bed, and Hastings and I will summon a doctor.
context: Poirot diagnoses strain and fever and arranges medical help.

text: Shall we try our old friend, the Hôtel des Bains?
context: Poirot proposes returning to the Hôtel des Bains.

text: Has an English lady, Miss Robinson, arrived?
context: Poirot asks whether the disguised Dulcie has arrived.

text: It is that I have arranged you a marriage, Hastings.
context: Poirot tells Hastings he has arranged his marriage.

text: Do you think I wish to trumpet aloud in Merlinville the name of Duveen?
context: Poirot explains why he used an alias for Duveen.

text: for the moment we have no time for sentiment. There is work ahead of us. Mademoiselle, were you able to do what I asked you?
context: Poirot suppresses sentiment and turns the group to work.

text: I am pleased with you. Go now and rest yourself. Hastings here and I have work to do. You shall see him tomorrow.
context: Poirot praises Dulcie and sends her to rest.

text: Come then, mademoiselle. But it will not be amusing. In all probability nothing will happen.
context: Poirot permits Dulcie to accompany them while warning nothing may happen.

text: I should like to assure myself that all goes well with Jack Renauld. Come with me, Hastings. Mademoiselle will perhaps remain outside. Madame Daubreuil might say something which would wound her.
context: Poirot explains his plan to check on Jack while protecting Dulcie from hurtful words.

text: I figure to myself that that is the room where we shall find Jack Renauld.
context: Poirot infers which room contains Jack.

text: Is the doctor coming again?
context: Poirot quietly asks whether the doctor will return.

text: M. Stonor has not been in Merlinville today, has he?
context: Poirot casually asks whether Stonor has been in Merlinville.

text: I thought you might have seen him coming or going, that is all. Good night, madame.
context: Poirot explains his ostensible reason and bids Madame Daubreuil good night.

text: No '_whys___,' Hastings. There will be time for that later.
context: Poirot postpones Hastings's questions.

text: She must have changed her room!
context: Poirot realizes Madame Renauld changed rooms.

text: And it will take time to burst it open.
context: Poirot calculates that forcing the door will take time.

text: You forget. She's a professional acrobat, Hastings. It was the providence of the good God that made her insist on coming with us tonight. I only pray that she may be in time. Ah!
context: Poirot trusts Dulcie's acrobatic skill and prays she arrives in time.

text: By the window, whilst we were battering at the door. Where is—the other?
context: Poirot identifies the silk ladder as an escape route.

text: The murderer of M. Renauld, Hastings. And the would-be murderer of Madame Renauld.
context: Poirot identifies the dead woman as Renauld's murderer and Madame Renauld's attacker.

text: I demand you—why—was—I—not—told? You are an old woman completely imbecile! And Léonie and Denise are no better. All of you are triple idiots! Your stupidity has nearly caused the death of your mistress.
context: Poirot berates the servants for endangering Madame Renauld.

text: _Enfin___ you wake! But it is that you are a famous sleeper, Hastings! Do you know that it is nearly eleven o'clock?
context: Poirot wakes Hastings and teases him for sleeping late.

text: You were not dreaming. All that is quite true.
context: Poirot confirms that Hastings's apparent dream was real.

text: Oh, no, Hastings, she did not! She said she did—yes—but that was to save the man she loved from the guillotine.
context: Poirot explains that Bella falsely confessed to save Jack.

text: Remember Jack Renauld's story. They both arrived on the scene at the same instant, and each took the other to be the perpetrator of the crime. The girl stares at him in horror, and then with a cry rushes away. But, when she hears that the crime has been brought home to him, she cannot bear it, and comes forward to accuse herself and save him from certain death.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Jack and Bella each mistook the other for the killer.

text: All along I was strongly under the impression that we were dealing with a cold-blooded and premeditated crime committed by some one who had been contented (very cleverly) with using M. Renauld's own plans for throwing the police off the track. The great criminal (as you may remember my remarking to you once) is always supremely simple.
context: Poirot explains why the case always seemed like a cold-blooded use of Renauld's own plan.

text: Now, to support this theory, the criminal must have been fully cognizant of Mr. Renauld's plans. That leads us to Madame Renauld. But facts fail to support any theory of her guilt. Is there any one else who might have known of them? Yes.
context: Poirot reasons that the killer had to know Renauld's plan and eliminates Madame Renauld.

text: From Marthe Daubreuil's own lips we have the admission that she overheard M. Renauld's quarrel with the tramp. If she could overhear that, there is no reason why she should not have heard everything else, especially if M. and Madame Renauld were imprudent enough to discuss their plans sitting on the bench. Remember how easily you overheard Marthe's conversation with Jack Renauld from that spot.
context: Poirot explains how Marthe could have overheard the entire plan.

text: What motive? Money! M. Renauld was a millionaire several times over, and at his death (or so she and Jack believed) half that vast fortune would pass to his son. Let us reconstruct the scene from the standpoint of Marthe Daubreuil.
context: Poirot identifies money as Marthe's motive and begins her reconstruction.

text: Marthe Daubreuil overhears what passes between Renauld and his wife. So far he has been a nice little source of income to the Daubreuil mother and daughter, but now he proposes to escape from their toils. At first, possibly, her idea is to prevent that escape. But a bolder idea takes its place, and one that fails to horrify the daughter of Jeanne Beroldy! At present M. Renauld stands inexorably in the way of her marriage with Jack.
context: Poirot explains how Marthe learned the plan and decided to exploit it.

text: If the latter defies his father, he will be a pauper—which is not at all to the mind of Mademoiselle Marthe. In fact, I doubt if she has ever cared a straw for Jack Renauld. She can simulate emotion, but in reality she is of the same cold, calculating type as her mother. I doubt, too, whether she was really very sure of her hold over the boy's affections. She had dazzled and captivated him, but separated from her, as his father could so easily manage to separate him, she might lose him.
context: Poirot analyzes Marthe's calculated interest in Jack and Renauld's fortune.

text: But with M. Renauld dead, and Jack the heir to half his millions, the marriage can take place at once, and at a stroke she will attain wealth—not the beggarly thousands that have been extracted from him so far. And her clever brain takes in the simplicity of the thing. It is all so easy. M. Renauld is planning all the circumstances of his death—she has only to step in at the right moment and turn the farce into a grim reality. And here comes in the second point which led me infallibly to Marthe Daubreuil—the dagger! Jack Renauld had _three___ souvenirs made.
context: Poirot explains how Marthe could turn Renauld's staged death into a real one.

text: One he gave to his mother, one to Bella Duveen; was it not highly probable that he had given the third one to Marthe Daubreuil?
context: Poirot connects the third souvenir dagger to Marthe.

text: (1) Marthe Daubreuil could have overheard M. Renauld's plans.
context: Poirot states that Marthe could overhear Renauld's plan.

text: (2) Marthe Daubreuil had a direct interest in causing M. Renauld's death.
context: Poirot states that Marthe directly benefited from Renauld's death.

text: (3) Marthe Daubreuil was the daughter of the notorious Madame Beroldy who in my opinion was morally and virtually the murderess of her husband, although it may have been Georges Conneau's hand which struck the actual blow.
context: Poirot cites Marthe's parentage and Madame Beroldy's moral guilt.

text: (4) Marthe Daubreuil was the only person, besides Jack Renauld, likely to have the third dagger in her possession.
context: Poirot states that Marthe was the likely owner of the third dagger.

text: Of course, when I learned of the existence of the other girl, Bella Duveen, I realized that it was quite possible that _she___ might have killed M. Renauld. The solution did not commend itself to me, because, as I pointed out to you, Hastings, an expert, such as I am, likes to meet a foeman worthy of his steel. Still one must take crimes as one finds them, not as one would like them to be. It did not seem very likely that Bella Duveen would be wandering about carrying a souvenir paper-knife in her hand, but of course she might have had some idea all the time of revenging herself on Jack Renauld. When she actually came forward and confessed to the murder, it seemed that all was over.
context: Poirot admits Bella was a possible killer but disliked the inelegant solution.

text: And yet—I was not satisfied, _mon ami___. _I was not satisfied.…___
context: Poirot says Bella's confession still failed to satisfy him.

text: I went over the case again minutely, and I came to the same conclusion as before. If it was _not___ Bella Duveen, the only other person who could have committed the crime was Marthe Daubreuil. But I had not one single proof against her!
context: Poirot explains that Marthe remained the only alternative but lacked proof.

text: And then you showed me that letter from Mademoiselle Dulcie, and I saw a chance of settling the matter once for all. The original dagger was stolen by Dulcie Duveen and thrown into the sea—since, as she thought, it belonged to her sister. But if, by any chance, it was _not___ her sister's, but the one given by Jack to Marthe Daubreuil—why then, Bella Duveen's dagger would be still intact! I said no word to you, Hastings (it was no time for romance) but I sought out Mademoiselle Dulcie, told her as much as I deemed needful, and set her to search amongst the effects of her sister. Imagine my elation, when she sought me out (according to my instructions) as Miss Robinson with the precious souvenir in her possession!
context: Poirot explains how Dulcie's letter and the surviving dagger could test Marthe's guilt.

text: In the meantime I had taken steps to force Mademoiselle Marthe into the open. By my orders, Mrs. Renauld repulsed her son, and declared her intention of making a will on the morrow which should cut him off from ever enjoying even a portion of his father's fortune. It was a desperate step, but a necessary one, and Madame Renauld was fully prepared to take the risk—though unfortunately she also never thought of mentioning her change of room. I suppose she took it for granted that I knew. All happened as I thought.
context: Poirot explains how he forced Marthe into action through Madame Renauld's staged rejection.

text: Marthe Daubreuil made a last bold bid for the Renauld millions—and failed!
context: Poirot concludes that Marthe's final attempt failed.

text: Ah, but we did not leave her behind. She was out of the Villa Marguerite by the back way whilst we were talking to her mother in the hall. That is where, as the Americans say, she 'put it over' on Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot admits Marthe escaped the Villa Marguerite before they left.

text: _Eh bien___, when we looked up, Madame Daubreuil had just had time to run upstairs and take her place.
context: Poirot explains that Madame Daubreuil created the silhouette in the window.

text: Yes. One is old, and one is young, one dark, and one fair, but, for the purpose of a silhouette on a blind, their profiles are singularly alike. Even I did not suspect—triple imbecile that I was! I thought I had plenty of time before me—that she would not try to gain admission to the Villa until much later. She had brains, that beautiful Mademoiselle Marthe.
context: Poirot explains the similar profiles and admits Marthe outwitted him.

text: Yes. The whole fortune would then pass to her son. But it would have been suicide, _mon ami!___ On the floor by Marthe Daubreuil's body, I found a pad and a little bottle of chloroform and a hypodermic syringe containing a fatal dose of morphine. You understand? The chloroform first—then when the victim is unconscious the prick of the needle.
context: Poirot explains Marthe's plan to make Madame Renauld's murder look like suicide.

text: By the morning the smell of the chloroform has quite disappeared, and the syringe lies where it has fallen from Madame Renauld's hand. What would he say, the excellent M. Hautet? 'Poor woman! What did I tell you? The shock of joy, it was too much on top of the rest!
context: Poirot reconstructs the chloroform, morphine, and expected official conclusion.

text: Did I not say that I should not be surprised if her brain became unhinged. Altogether a most tragic case, the Renauld Case!'
context: Poirot reconstructs the chloroform, morphine, and expected official conclusion.

text: However, Hastings, things did not go quite as Mademoiselle Marthe had planned. To begin with, Madame Renauld was awake and waiting for her. There is a struggle. But Madame Renauld is terribly weak still. There is a last chance for Marthe Daubreuil.
context: Poirot reconstructs Marthe's struggle with Madame Renauld.

text: The idea of suicide is at an end, but if she can silence Madame Renauld with her strong hands, make a getaway with her little silk ladder whilst we are still battering on the inside of the further door, and be back at the Villa Marguerite before we return there, it will be hard to prove anything against her. But she was checkmated—not by Hercule Poirot—but by _la petite acrobate___ with her wrists of steel.
context: Poirot explains Marthe's attempted escape and Dulcie's intervention.

text: My friend, do you remember when we drove into Merlinville that first day? And the beautiful girl we saw standing at the gate? You asked me if I had not noticed a young goddess, and I replied to you that I had seen only a girl with anxious eyes. That is how I have thought of Marthe Daubreuil from the beginning. _The girl with the anxious eyes!___ Why was she anxious?
context: Poirot recalls first seeing Marthe as the girl with anxious eyes.

text: Not on Jack Renauld's behalf, for she did not know then that he had been in Merlinville the previous evening.
context: Poirot asks what caused Marthe's anxiety before Jack was suspected.

text: Much better. He is still at the Villa Marguerite. But Madame Daubreuil has disappeared. The police are looking for her.
context: Poirot reports Jack's recovery and Madame Daubreuil's disappearance.

text: We shall never know. Madame is a lady who can keep her secrets. And I doubt very much if the police will ever find her.
context: Poirot predicts Madame Daubreuil will keep her secrets and evade police.

text: Naturally. And yet, do you know, Hastings, I doubt if his heart was ever seriously engaged. So far we have looked upon Bella Duveen as a siren, and Marthe Daubreuil as the girl he really loved. But I think that if we reversed the terms we should come nearer to the truth. Marthe Daubreuil was very beautiful.
context: Poirot re-evaluates Jack's feelings for Bella and Marthe.

text: She set herself to fascinate Jack, and she succeeded, but remember his curious reluctance to break with the other girl. And see how he was willing to go to the guillotine rather than implicate her. I have a little idea that when he learns the truth he will be horrified—revolted, and his false love will wither away.
context: Poirot predicts Jack's false love for Marthe will die when he learns the truth.

text: He has a _crise___ of the nerves, that one! He has been obliged to return to Paris.
context: Poirot jokes that Giraud has suffered a nervous crisis.

text: To hide the truth, never does it avail, madame! Be brave and tell him everything.
context: Poirot urges Madame Renauld to tell Jack the truth.

text: Reassure yourself, M. Jack. The world knows nothing. As far as I can see, there is no obligation for me to take the police into my confidence. Throughout the case I have acted, not for them, but for your father. Justice overtook him at last, but no one need ever know that he and Georges Conneau were one and the same.
context: Poirot assures Jack that his father's secret need not become public.

text: _Mais, oui!___ I got my 500 francs! Is he not a splendid fellow? I call him Giraud!
context: Poirot reveals that he won five hundred francs from Giraud.

text: You go alone, M. Jack?
context: Poirot asks Jack whether he will travel alone.

text: A girl who loves you very dearly—who has been willing to lay down her life for you.
context: Poirot reminds Jack of the girl willing to die for him.

text: _Les femmes___—they have a wonderful genius for manufacturing crutches for stories like that.
context: Poirot says women can invent support for an awkward story.

text: Yes. You are your mother's son as well as your father's. Go then to Mademoiselle Bella. Tell her everything. Keep nothing back—and see what she will say!
context: Poirot tells Jack to confess everything to Bella.

text: Go to her as a boy no longer, but a man—a man bowed by the fate of the Past, and the fate of Today, but looking forward to a new and wonderful life. Ask her to share it with you. You may not realize it, but your love for each other has been tested in the fire and not found wanting. You have both been willing to lay down your lives for each other.
context: Poirot urges Jack to ask Bella to share a new life after their mutual sacrifice.

text: As usual, your facts are tinged with your incurable romanticism. That is Miss Mary Marvell, the film star. She is being followed by a bevy of admirers who have recognized her. And, *en passant*, my dear Hastings, she is quite aware of the fact!
context: Poirot responds to Hastings while they watch Mary Marvell approach his rooms.

text: Ah! *Sacré*! Is it that you expect her to promenade herself in the streets of London in a cowboy hat, or with bare feet, and a bunch of curls, as an Irish colleen? Always with you it is the non-essentials! Remember the case of the dancer, Valerie Saintclair.
context: Poirot responds to Hastings while they watch Mary Marvell approach his rooms.

text: Very simply. This street, it is not aristocratic, *mon ami*! In it there is no fashionable doctor, no fashionable dentist—still less is there a fashionable milliner! But there *is* a fashionable detective. *Oui*, my friend, it is true—I am become the mode, the *dernier cri*!
context: Poirot responds to Hastings while they watch Mary Marvell approach his rooms.

text: Not now, I pray you. Let us not confuse the mind. And observe that 'Peerage'—how you have replaced him! See you not that the tallest books go in the top shelf, the next tallest in the row beneath, and so on. Thus we have order, *method*, which, as I have often told you, Hastings——
context: Poirot analyzes the Yardly situation and directs Lord Yardly's next steps.

text: *Mais oui!* The warning letters, the Chinaman, the article in *Society Gossip*, all sprang from the ingenious brain of Mr. Rolf! The two diamonds, supposed to be so miraculously alike—bah! they did not exist. There was only *one* diamond, my friend!
context: Poirot explains how Gregory Rolf and Lady Yardly staged the diamond affair.

text: I demand pardon, But, if my memory is not at fault, in the case of a recent murder, the doctor first gave a verdict of heart failure—altering it when the local constable pointed out that there was a bullet wound through the head!
context: Poirot discusses the insurance case and questions Dr. Bernard about Maltravers's death.

text: A slight discrepancy, that is all! You noticed it? You did not? Still, life is full of discrepancies, and assuredly the man cannot have taken his own life—there is no poison that would fill his mouth with blood. No, no, I must resign myself to the fact that all here is clear and above-board—but who is this?
context: Poirot questions Mrs. Maltravers and notices inconsistencies in her account.

text: I will assist you. To begin with, Black answered well within the normal time limit, with no pauses, so we can take it that he himself has no guilty knowledge to conceal. 'Day' to 'Night' and 'Place' to 'Name' are normal associations. I began work with 'Bernard' which might have suggested the local doctor had he come across him at all. Evidently he had not.
context: Poirot interviews Captain Black and analyzes his word associations.

text: You see, there was a little discrepancy. The doctor seemed to think the deceased was a Christian Scientist, and who could have given him that impression but Mrs. Maltravers? But to us she represented him as being in a grave state of apprehension about his own health. Again, why was she so taken aback by the reappearance of young Black?
context: Poirot explains how Mrs. Maltravers murdered her husband.

text: And lastly, although I know that convention decrees that a woman must make a decent pretence of mourning for her husband, I do not care for such heavily-rouged eyelids! You did not observe them, Hastings? No? As I always tell you, you see nothing!
context: Poirot explains how Mrs. Maltravers murdered her husband.

text: I am most serious. Figure to yourself, my friend, that the real rent of those flats is £350. I have just ascertained that from the landlord's agents. And yet this particular flat is being sublet at eighty pounds! Why?
context: Poirot analyzes the suspiciously cheap flat and questions Hastings about the Robinsons.

text: *Tout doucement, mon ami*. Have I said that I think anything? All I say is—it is a curious story. And there is nothing to throw light upon it; except perhaps the lady's name, eh, Hastings?
context: Poirot analyzes the suspiciously cheap flat and questions Hastings about the Robinsons.

text: We are about to descend after the method of the dustbins, No one will observe us. The Sunday concert, the Sunday 'afternoon out,' and finally the Sunday nap after the Sunday dinner of England—*le rosbif*—all these will distract attention from the doings of Hercule Poirot. Come, my friend.
context: Poirot prepares to investigate Montagu Mansions.

text: Observation was always your strong point, Hastings! Nothing escapes you. No, the man is not a Jap. He is an Italian.
context: Poirot directs the nighttime trap at the flat and confronts the intruders.

text: Ah! *Sapristi*, but exactly! That was the point. Elsa Hardt and her husband, or brother or whatever he really is, come from New York, and take a flat in the name of Mr. and Mrs. Robinson. Suddenly they learn that one of these secret societies, the Mafia, or the Camorra, to which doubtless Luigi Valdarno belonged, is on their track.
context: Poirot reveals the spy scheme and explains the significance of the Robinson name.

text: Well, why not? You should know my methods by now. All I ask is that you should report to me fully every day, and follow implicitly any instructions I may wire you.
context: Poirot, recovering from influenza, sends Hastings to investigate Hunter's Lodge.

text: Your illuminating answers to my wires supplied me with the truth. See here, Hastings, let us examine the facts methodically and in order. Mr. Harrington Pace is a man with a considerable fortune which at his death will doubtless pass to his nephew. Point No. 1.
context: Poirot explains how Roger and Zoe Havering committed the murder.

text: Oh, no, that was not her part! Her part was what you have just mentioned, to provide an alibi for Mrs. Havering at the moment the shot was fired. And no one will ever find her, *mon ami*, because she does not exist! 'There's no sech person,' as your so great Shakespeare says.
context: Poirot explains how Roger and Zoe Havering committed the murder.

text: I mean that Zoe Havering was an actress before her marriage, that you and Japp only saw the housekeeper in a dark hall, a dim middle-aged figure in black with a faint subdued voice, and finally that neither you nor Japp, nor the local police whom the housekeeper fetched, ever saw Mrs. Middleton and her mistress at one and the same time. It was child's play for that clever and daring woman. On the pretext of summoning her mistress, she runs upstairs, slips on a bright jumper and a hat with black curls attached which she jams down over the grey transformation. A few deft touches, and the make-up is removed, a slight dusting of rouge, and the brilliant Zoe Havering comes down with her clear ringing voice.
context: Poirot explains how Roger and Zoe Havering committed the murder.

text: No, that was Roger Havering's job—but it was a mistake on their part. It put me on the right track. A man who has committed a murder with a revolver which he found on the spot would fling it away at once, he would not carry it up to London with him. No, the motive was clear, the criminals wished to focus the interest of the police on a spot far removed from Derbyshire they were anxious to get the police away as soon as possible from the vicinity of Hunter's Lodge.
context: Poirot explains how Roger and Zoe Havering committed the murder.

text: Curious, Very curious! They waste much, much time trying to prise it open, and then—*sapristi!* they find that they have the key all the time—for each of Hubbs's locks are unique.
context: Poirot questions Philip Ridgeway and the bank managers about the missing bonds.

text: Ah! there is exactly the question we are asking ourselves! I venture to prophesy that the solution, if we ever find it, will hinge on that curious fact. I beg of you not to assault me if I ask you one more question: *Are you perfectly certain you did not leave the trunk unlocked?*
context: Poirot questions Philip Ridgeway and the bank managers about the missing bonds.

text: To a South Sea island where there is no extradition? No, *mon ami*, he would find life very uncongenial there. As to why I wait—*eh bien* to the intelligence of Hercule Poirot the case is perfectly clear, but for the benefit of others, not so greatly gifted by the good God—the Inspector McNeil, for instance—it would be as well to make a few inquiries to establish the facts. One must have consideration for those less gifted than oneself.
context: Poirot tells Hastings the bond case is simpler than it appears.

text: *Jamais de la vie!* If the package was thrown overboard, and the bonds were sold in New York, the package could not have contained the bonds. Is there any evidence that the package *did* contain the bonds? Remember, Mr. Ridgeway never opened it from the time it was placed in his hands in London.
context: Poirot reconstructs how the bonds were sent to New York and identifies the thief.

text: The man who had a duplicate key, the man who ordered the lock, the man who has *not* been severely ill with bronchitis at his home in the country—*enfin*, that 'stodgy' old man, Mr. Shaw! There are criminals in high places sometimes, my friend. Ah, here we are. Mademoiselle, I have succeeded! You permit?
context: Poirot reconstructs how the bonds were sent to New York and identifies the thief.

text: No, indeed, Lady Willard, I, too, believe in the force of superstition, one of the greatest forces the world has ever known.
context: Poirot discusses the supposed Egyptian curse with Lady Willard.

text: And my boots, Regard them, Hastings. My boots, of the neat patent leather, usually so smart and shining. See, the sand is inside them, which is painful, and outside them, which outrages the eyesight. Also the heat, it causes my moustaches to become limp—but limp!
context: Poirot travels to Egypt and reacts to the conditions and another death.

text: Was there no science then in Ancient Egypt? No, no, do not answer me, but tell me this. What do the native workmen think?
context: Poirot questions the expedition and observes the suspects at the excavation.

text: We cannot be sure. He knew young Bleibner too, remember, and may have suspected something, or, again, the doctor may have thought that a further death motiveless and purposeless would strengthen the coils of superstition. Furthermore, I will tell you an interesting psychological fact, Hastings. A murderer has always a strong desire to repeat his successful crime, the performance of it grows upon him.
context: Poirot exposes Dr. Ames and explains the murders connected with the tomb.

text: Hence my fears for young Willard. The figure of Anubis you saw to-night was Hassan, dressed up by my orders. I wanted to see if I could frighten the doctor. But it would take more than the supernatural to frighten him.
context: Poirot exposes Dr. Ames and explains the murders connected with the tomb.

text: The sight of so many jewels makes me wish I had turned my brains to crime, instead of to its detection. What a magnificent opportunity for some thief of distinction! Regard, Hastings, that stout woman by the pillar. She is, as you would say, plastered with gems.
context: Poirot and Hastings discuss the jewels at the Grand Metropolitan.

text: *Si, si, mon enfant*—I assure you it is better not to resist. You permit, monsieur? A little experiment—purely for my own satisfaction.
context: Poirot calms Mrs. Opalsen and tests the timing of the pearl theft.

text: Now observe my actions. Madame will have the kindness to give me the key of the jewel-case. I thank you. My friend Hastings will have the kindness to say 'Go!'
context: Poirot calms Mrs. Opalsen and tests the timing of the pearl theft.

text: *Une blague!* For the benefit of the valet. The card was one with a specially prepared surface—for finger-prints. I went straight to Scotland Yard, asked for our old friend Inspector Japp, and laid the facts before him. As I had suspected, the finger-prints proved to be those of two well-known jewel thieves who have been 'wanted' for some time.
context: Poirot explains how the chambermaid and valet stole the pearls.

text: *Enfantillage!* One can hardly take it seriously. To fire with the rifle—never does it succeed. It is a device of the past.
context: Poirot receives the urgent case of the kidnapped Prime Minister.

text: You have the word of Hercule Poirot—I can say no more!
context: Poirot receives the urgent case of the kidnapped Prime Minister.

text: Amen to that! Ah, but my Master does not forget.... Messieurs, I, Hercule Poirot, will serve you faithfully.
context: Poirot pledges to serve after learning who recommended him for the Prime Minister case.

text: For: that his promptness in restarting the car saved the Premier's life; that he is a Scotland Yard man, and, obviously, from the post allotted to him, a trusted detective. Now for Daniels. There is not much against him, except the fact that nothing is known of his antecedents, and that he speaks too many languages for a good Englishman! (Pardon me, *mon ami*, but, as linguists, you are deplorable!) *Now for him*, we have the fact that he was found gagged, bound, and chloroformed—which does not look as though he had anything to do with the matter.
context: Poirot weighs the evidence against Daniels and O'Murphy.

text: All that matters is the little grey cells within. Secretly and silently they do their part, until suddenly I call for a map, and I lay my finger on a spot—so—and I say: the Prime Minister is *there*! And it is so! With method and logic one can accomplish anything!
context: Poirot follows the clues while traveling between England and France.

text: He and O'Murphy were driven straight to the house of 'Mrs. Everard,' at Hampstead, Daniels' so-called 'aunt.' In reality, she is Frau Bertha Ebenthal, and the police have been looking for her for some time. It is a valuable little present that I have made to them—to say nothing of Daniels! Ah, it was a clever plan, but he did not reckon on the cleverness of Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot reveals where the Prime Minister was kidnapped and how the plot worked.

text: By no means. These things are all good in their way. The danger is they may assume undue importance. Most details are insignificant; one or two are vital.
context: Poirot accepts Japp's challenge to solve Davenheim's disappearance without leaving his chair.

text: It is the brain, the little grey cells on which one must rely. The senses mislead. One must seek the truth within—not without.
context: Poirot accepts Japp's challenge to solve Davenheim's disappearance without leaving his chair.

text: *Eh bien, mon ami*, I accept. *Le sport*, it is the passion of you English. Now—the facts.
context: Poirot accepts Japp's challenge to solve Davenheim's disappearance without leaving his chair.

text: It seemed almost certain that it was arranged for—himself! Then his passion of late years for buying jewellery! How simple! The funds he embezzled, he converted into jewels, very likely replacing them in turn with paste duplicates, and so he put away in a safe place, under another name, a considerable fortune to be enjoyed all in good time when every one has been thrown off the track.
context: Poirot explains Davenheim's preparations, then becomes distracted by breakfast-table disorder.

text: You are seeking Monsieur Davenheim in order to put him in prison, so you never dream of looking to see if he may not be already there!
context: Poirot explains Davenheim's disguise and hiding place.

text: *Mon ami*, you miss the point. I am looking for something that I do *not* see.
context: Poirot investigates Count Foscatini's apartment and the uneaten dinner.

text: Monsieur, explain to me, I pray, your system of serving meals here.
context: Poirot investigates Count Foscatini's apartment and the uneaten dinner.

text: Not exactly, I am more interested in Count Foscatini's appetite. Did he partake of every dish?
context: Poirot investigates Count Foscatini's apartment and the uneaten dinner.

text: I have employed my grey cells to some advantage. Come, Signor Ascanio, you visited the dead man on the Tuesday morning—that is so, is it not?
context: Poirot challenges the official solution and questions Signor Ascanio.

text: Strange, We all have the little grey cells. And so few of us know how to use them. Good morning, Signor Ascanio.
context: Poirot challenges the official solution and questions Signor Ascanio.

text: It bears that interpretation, certainly, Somewhere in this rambling old manor-house your uncle has concealed either a sum of money in notes or possibly a second will, and has given you a year in which to exercise your ingenuity to find it.
context: Poirot accepts Violet Marsh's challenge to find her uncle's hidden will.

text: Made three years ago. Dated March 25; and the time is given also—11 a.m.—that is very suggestive. It narrows the field of search. Assuredly it is another will we have to seek for.
context: Poirot accepts Violet Marsh's challenge to find her uncle's hidden will.

text: A will made even half-an-hour later would upset this. *Eh bien*, mademoiselle, it is a problem charming and ingenious that you have presented to me here. I shall have all the pleasure in the world in solving it for you. Granted that your uncle was a man of ability, his grey cells cannot have been of the quality of Hercule Poirot's!
context: Poirot accepts Violet Marsh's challenge to find her uncle's hidden will.

text: *Eh bien, mon ami*, we must map out our plan of campaign. Already I have made a rough survey of the house, but I am of opinion that any clue will be found in this room. We shall have to go through the documents in the desk with meticulous care.
context: Poirot searches Crabtree Manor methodically for clues to the will.

text: Imbecile that I have been! Triple imbecile! Not again will I vaunt my little grey cells!
context: Poirot realizes the final trick in Andrew Marsh's hidden-will puzzle.

text: But no, Hastings. It is *your* wits that go astray. Miss Marsh proved the astuteness of her wits and the value of the higher education for women by at once putting the matter in *my* hands. Always employ the expert. She has amply proved her right to the money.
context: Poirot realizes the final trick in Andrew Marsh's hidden-will puzzle.

text: You see now, my friend? Or if you do not, I will add this. Mrs. Rupert Carrington was, before her marriage, Flossie Halliday, daughter of old man Halliday, the steel king of America.
context: Poirot explains to Hastings why Halliday's note concerns the train murder.

text: I did him a little service in the past—an affair of bearer bonds. And once, when I was in Paris for a royal visit, I had Mademoiselle Flossie pointed out to me. *La jolie petite pensionnaire!* She had the *jolie dot* too! It caused trouble.
context: Poirot recalls his prior connection to Halliday and Flossie.

text: A certain Count de la Rochefour. *Un bien mauvais sujet!* A bad hat, as you would say. An adventurer pure and simple, who knew how to appeal to a romantic young girl. Luckily her father got wind of it in time.
context: Poirot describes the Count's character and influence on Flossie.

text: Ah, the poor little lady! *Elle n'est pas bien tombée!*
context: Poirot reacts sympathetically to Flossie's unhappy marriage.

text: Old man Halliday is no fool. He would tie up her money pretty tight.
context: Poirot predicts that Halliday protected his daughter's fortune.

text: My good friend, do not jump down my throat like that. You are interested, I see. Supposing you accompany me to see Mr. Halliday. There is a taxi stand at the corner.
context: Poirot calms Hastings and invites him to meet Halliday.

text: I accept, monsieur, all the more willingly that I saw your daughter in Paris several times. And now I will ask you to tell me the circumstances of her journey to Plymouth and any other details that seem to you to bear upon the case.
context: Poirot accepts Halliday's case and requests the relevant facts.

text: Who had charge of the jewels? Your daughter, or the maid?
context: Poirot interrupts Halliday to establish who carried the jewels.

text: Is there nothing to account for your daughter's sudden change of plan?
context: Poirot asks Halliday what might explain Flossie's altered journey.

text: The train was a corridor one, of course?
context: Poirot checks the physical arrangement of the train.

text: Which side was the corridor?
context: Poirot asks which side of the carriage the corridor occupied.

text: It affects my nerves to see anything crooked. Strange, is it not? I was saying, monsieur, that there is no doubt in your mind, as to this probably unexpected meeting being the cause of your daughter's sudden change of plan?
context: After straightening an inkstand, Poirot resumes questioning Halliday.

text: One thing more, monsieur. Your daughter's fortune—to whom does it pass at her death?
context: Poirot asks Halliday who benefits financially from Flossie's death.

text: You will permit me to put a few questions? Your mistress, she was quite as usual before starting yesterday morning? Not excited or flurried?
context: Poirot begins interviewing Jane Mason about Flossie's behavior.

text: It was not your master, Mr. Carrington, by any chance?
context: Poirot asks whether the unidentified man could have been Carrington.

text: Would it be possible for the man to have entered the train at Bristol before you reached the carriage?
context: Poirot tests Jane Mason's account of the mysterious man's arrival.

text: The papers give a few details, but I would like you to confirm them.
context: Poirot asks Jane Mason to verify Flossie's clothing.

text: *Précisément!* —Thank you, mademoiselle.
context: Poirot acknowledges the clothing description and dismisses the maid politely.

text: That is all I can do here—except, monsieur, that I would ask you to tell me everything—but *everything*!
context: Poirot presses Halliday to disclose every relevant fact.

text: Then there is nothing more to be said. I must decline the case.
context: Poirot threatens to withdraw when Halliday insists he has told everything.

text: Because you have not been frank with me.
context: Poirot explains why he will not proceed without full disclosure.

text: No, you are keeping something back.
context: Poirot directly challenges Halliday's concealment.

text: I fancy, monsieur, that you did not know that your daughter intended renewing her acquaintance with the Count de la Rochefour?
context: Poirot confronts Halliday with the significance of the hidden letter.

text: Monsieur, I did not. But to track footmarks, and recognize cigarette-ash is not sufficient for a detective. He must also be a good psychologist! I knew that you disliked and mistrusted your son-in-law. He benefits by your daughter's death; the maid's description of the mysterious man bears a sufficient resemblance to him.
context: Poirot explains how psychology revealed that Halliday was withholding evidence.

text: Yet you are not keen on his track! Why? Surely because your suspicions lie in another direction. Therefore you were keeping something back.
context: Poirot completes his inference from Halliday's reluctance to accuse Carrington.

text: Yes. The Count says: 'Before very long, and perhaps sooner than you think.' Obviously he would not want to wait until you should get wind of his reappearance. Was it he who traveled down from London by the twelve-fourteen, and came along the corridor to your daughter's compartment? The Count de la Rochefour is also, if I remember rightly, tall and dark!
context: Poirot considers whether the Count arranged the meeting on the train.

text: Well, monsieur, I will wish you good day. Scotland Yard, has, I presume, a list of the jewels?
context: Poirot closes the interview and checks whether police have the jewel list.

text: They function, my good Japp; assuredly they do!
context: Poirot answers Japp's teasing question about his little gray cells.

text: A great precaution, but perhaps a day late,
context: Poirot gently mocks Japp's decision to watch Carrington.

text: You will come round and see me this evening, and tell me the result?
context: Poirot asks Japp to report the results of his inquiries.

text: He travels; he measures footprints; he collects mud and cigarette-ash! He is extremely busy! He is zealous beyond words! And if I mentioned psychology to him, do you know what he would do, my friend?
context: Poirot contrasts Japp's physical investigations with his own psychological method.

text: He would smile! He would say to himself: 'Poor old Poirot! He ages! He grows senile!'
context: Poirot imagines Japp dismissing his psychological approach.

text: Japp is the 'younger generation knocking on the door.' And *ma foi!* They are so busy knocking that they do not notice that the door is open!
context: Poirot jokes to Hastings about Japp and the younger generation.

text: As we have *carte blanche*, I shall expend threepence in ringing up the Ritz—where you may have noticed our Count is staying. After that, as my feet are a little damp, and I have sneezed twice, I shall return to my rooms and make myself a *tisano* over the spirit lamp!
context: Poirot states his next investigative step and his concern for his health.

text: Because the Count de la Rochefour has left the Ritz? You go too fast, my friend.
context: Poirot cautions Hastings against clearing Carrington too quickly.

text: One hundred thousand dollars' worth of jewelry is a very good motive for anyone. No, the question to my mind is: why kill her? Why not simply steal the jewels? She would not prosecute.
context: Poirot distinguishes the motive for theft from the motive for murder.

text: Because she is a woman, *mon ami*. She once loved this man. Therefore she would suffer her loss in silence. And the Count, who is an extremely good psychologist where women are concerned,—hence his successes,—would know that perfectly well! On the other hand, if Rupert Carrington killed her, why take the jewels, which would incriminate him fatally?
context: Poirot reasons about Flossie's likely response to theft and the suspects' motives.

text: Me, I have arranged my ideas,
context: Poirot tells Japp that he has organized his thoughts.

text: *Quel dommage?*
context: Poirot responds ironically to Japp's remark about active young investigators.

text: You permit me to make a guess? You have found the knife with which the crime was committed by the side of the line between Weston and Taunton, and you have interviewed the paper-boy who spoke to Mrs. Carrington at Weston!
context: Poirot accurately predicts the results of Japp's investigation.

text: I am glad you admit for once that they are *all mighty*! Tell me, did she give the paper-boy a shilling for himself?
context: Poirot jokes about his gray cells and tests a detail of the paper-boy encounter.

text: And in consequence the boy did not forget her?
context: Poirot links the large tip to the paper-boy's clear memory.

text: I don't know—except that he was a short man.
context: Poirot infers the jewel passer's build before Japp names him.

text: Hastings, my hat. And the brush. So! My galoshes if it still rains!
context: Poirot directs Hastings to prepare his clothes before leaving.

text: We must not undo the good work of that *tisano*. Au revoir, Japp!
context: Poirot protects his health and takes leave of Japp.

text: Ask Mr. Halliday if he will be so kind as to mount to me here,
context: Poirot politely sends the footman to fetch Halliday upstairs.

text: I was looking, monsieur, for *this*.
context: Poirot produces the duplicate blue clothes from the maid's trunk.

text: If you will just shut the door, Hastings. Thank you. Yes, and stand with your back against it. Now, Mr. Halliday, let me introduce you to Grace Kidd, otherwise Jane Mason, who will shortly rejoin her accomplice, Red Narky, under the kind escort of Japp.
context: Poirot secures the room and reveals Jane Mason's true identity.

text: It was of the most simple.
context: Poirot characterizes his solution after exposing the accomplice.

text: It was the maid's insistence on the clothes that her mistress was wearing that first struck me. Why was she so anxious that our attention should be directed to them? I reflected that we had only the maid's word for the mysterious man in the carriage at Bristol. As far as the doctor's evidence went, Mrs. Carrington might easily have been murdered *before* reaching Bristol.
context: Poirot explains the first clues that made him distrust the maid's story.

text: But if so, then the maid must be an accomplice. And if she were an accomplice, she would not wish this point to rest on her evidence alone. The clothes Mrs. Carrington was wearing were of a striking nature. A maid usually has a good deal of choice as to what her mistress shall wear. Now if, after Bristol, anyone saw a lady in a bright blue coat and skirt, and a fur toque, he will be quite ready to swear he has seen Mrs. Carrington.
context: Poirot explains how the distinctive clothes were used to create false eyewitness evidence.

text: I began to reconstruct. The maid would provide herself with duplicate clothes. She and her accomplice chloroform and stab Mrs. Carrington between London and Bristol, probably taking advantage of a tunnel. Her body is rolled under the seat; the maid takes her place. At Weston she must make herself noticed.
context: Poirot reconstructs the murder and the maid's impersonation.

text: How? In all probability, a newspaper-boy will be selected. She will insure his remembering her by giving him a large tip. She also drew his attention to the color of her dress by a remark about one of the magazines. After leaving Weston, she throws the knife out of the window to mark the place where the crime presumably occurred, and changes her clothes, or buttons a long mackintosh over them.
context: Poirot explains how the disguised maid created a memorable sighting and false crime location.

text: At Taunton she leaves the train and returns to Bristol as soon as possible, where her accomplice has duly left the luggage in the cloak-room. He hands over the ticket and himself returns to London. She waits on the platform, carrying out her rôle, goes to a hotel for the night and returns to town in the morning exactly as she said.
context: Poirot completes the reconstruction of the accomplices' movements.

text: When Japp returned from his expedition, he confirmed all my deductions. He also told me that a well-known crook was passing the jewels. I knew that whoever it was would be the exact opposite of the man Jane Mason described. When I heard that it was Red Narky, who always worked with Gracie Kidd—well, I knew just where to find her.
context: Poirot explains how Japp's findings identified Jane Mason as Gracie Kidd.

text: The more I thought of it, the more I was convinced that he had nothing to do with it. That gentleman is much too careful of his own skin to risk murder. It would be out of keeping with his character.
context: Poirot clears the Count based on his assessment of the man's character.

text: The good Japp, he shall get the official credit, all right, but though he has got his Gracie Kidd, I think that I, as the Americans say, have got his goat!
context: Poirot privately jokes to Hastings about Japp receiving the official credit.

text: After all, it is possible that I shall not die this time.
context: Poirot comments on surviving influenza while convalescing.

text: Once more shall I be myself again, the great Hercule Poirot, the terror of evildoers! Figure to yourself, *mon ami*, that I have a little paragraph to myself in *Society Gossip*. But yes! Here it is!
context: Poirot boasts to Hastings about returning to form and mentions a society notice.

text: That is true. The few cases I have had to decline did not fill me with any regret.
context: Poirot says the cases declined during his illness were uninteresting.

text: Would it interest you, Hastings, to go down and hear what our visitor's particular trouble is? Make him all my excuses.
context: Poirot asks Hastings to interview the agitated visitor and convey his apologies.

text: I see—I see. You want to go yourself, is it not so? Well, why not? You should know my methods by now. All I ask is that you should report to me fully every day, and follow implicitly any instructions I may wire you.
context: Poirot authorizes Hastings to investigate in Derbyshire under his instructions.

text: *Mon ami Hastings!* But how glad I am to see you! Veritably I have for you a great affection!
context: Poirot warmly greets Hastings on his return from Derbyshire.

text: And you have enjoyed yourself? You have run to and fro with the good Japp? You have interrogated and investigated to your heart's content?
context: Poirot teasingly asks Hastings about his investigation with Japp.

text: It is true that we are not likely to cover ourselves with glory over it.
context: Poirot responds dryly when Hastings calls the case an unsolvable mystery.

text: Oh, as far as that goes, me, I am very good at cracking the nuts! A veritable squirrel! It is not that which embarrasses me. I know well enough who killed Mr. Harrington Pace.
context: Poirot boasts that solving the murder itself is not difficult.

text: Your illuminating answers to my wires supplied me with the truth..... See here, Hastings, let us examine the facts methodically and in order. Mr. Harrington Pace is a man with a considerable fortune which at his death will doubtless pass to his nephew—point number one. His nephew is known to be desperately hard up—point number two. His nephew is also known to be—shall we say a man of loose moral fiber? Point number three!
context: Poirot methodically lays out motive and character evidence against Roger Havering.

text: *Précisément!* And therefore, as Mr. Havering left Elmer's Dale at six-fifteen, and since Mr. Pace cannot have been killed before he left (or the doctor would have spotted the time of the crime as being given wrongly when he examined the body), we conclude, quite rightly, that Mr. Havering did *not* shoot his uncle. But there is a Mrs. Havering, Hastings.
context: Poirot reasons from Havering's verified journey to the possible involvement of Mrs. Havering.

text: Ah, yes, the housekeeper. But she has disappeared.
context: Poirot points out the disappearance of the supposed housekeeper.

text: I think not. There is something peculiarly elusive about that housekeeper—don't you think so? It struck me at once.
context: Poirot rejects Hastings's expectation that the housekeeper will be found.

text: Oh, no, that was not her part. Her part was what you have just mentioned, to provide an alibi for Mrs. Havering at the moment the shot was fired. And no one will ever find her, *mon ami*, because she does not exist! 'There's no sech person,' as your so great Shakespeare says.
context: Poirot explains that the housekeeper was invented to provide Mrs. Havering with an alibi.

text: I mean that Zoe Havering was an actress before her marriage, that you and Japp only saw the housekeeper in a dark hall, a dim, middle-aged figure in black with a faint, subdued voice, and finally that neither you, nor Japp, nor the local police whom the housekeeper fetched, ever saw Mrs. Middleton and her mistress at one and the same time. It was a child's play for that clever and daring woman. On the pretext of summoning her mistress, she runs upstairs, slips on a bright jumper and a hat with black curls attached which she jams down over the gray transformation. A few deft touches, and the make-up is removed; a slight dusting of rouge, and the brilliant Zoe Havering comes down with her clear ringing voice.
context: Poirot explains how Zoe Havering impersonated the housekeeper.

text: No, that was Roger Havering's job—but it was a mistake on their part. It put me on the right track. A man who has committed a murder with a revolver which he found on the spot would fling it away at once; he would not carry it up to London with him. No, the motive was clear; the criminals wished to focus the interest of the police on a spot far removed from Derbyshire; they were anxious to get the police away as soon as possible from the vicinity of Hunter's Lodge. Of course, the revolver found at Ealing was not the one with which Mr. Pace was shot.
context: Poirot explains why the revolver found in Ealing exposed the criminals' plan.

text: Roger Havering discharged one shot from it, brought it up to London, went straight to his club to establish his alibi, then went quickly out to Ealing by the District Railway, a matter of about twenty minutes only, placed the parcel where it was found and so back to town. That charming creature his wife, quietly shoots Mr. Pace after dinner—you remember he was shot from behind? Another significant point, that! She reloads the revolver and puts it back in its place, and then starts off with her desperate little comedy.
context: Poirot reconstructs Roger and Zoe Havering's actions during and after the murder.

text: And yet it is true. *Bien sûr*, my friend, it is true! But to bring that precious pair to justice, that is another matter. Well, Japp must do what he can—I have written him fully; but I very much fear, Hastings, that we shall be obliged to leave them to Fate—or *le bon Dieu*—whichever you prefer.
context: Poirot insists on the solution but doubts the murderers can be brought to justice.

text: But at a price, Hastings, always at a price, *croyez moi*!
context: Poirot answers Hastings's proverb with a warning about the price of wrongdoing.

text: If you please, mademoiselle.
