text: You comprehend me? But Captain Ralph Paton must be cleared—_ça va sans dire_. I should suggest that you finish that very interesting manuscript of yours—but abandoning your former reticence.
context: Poirot requires Ralph's exoneration and a truthful conclusion to Sheppard's manuscript.

text: Now that you remind me of the fact, it is true that there is one thing more. It would be most unwise on your part to attempt to silence me as you silenced M. Ackroyd. That kind of business does not succeed against Hercule Poirot, you understand.
context: Poirot warns Sheppard that an attempt to silence him would fail.

text: we leave that to Giraud and his friends. Hercule Poirot's methods are his own. Order and method, and 'the little gray cells.' Sitting at ease in our own arm-chairs we see the things that these others overlook, and we do not jump to the conclusion like the worthy Japp.
context: Poirot contrasts his methods with those of more conventional detectives.

text: *Mon Dieu!* but what a *chance épouvantable*. Regard around you, my friend.
context: Poirot reacts when Hastings arrives just as he is preparing to leave.

text: Speak not of it to me, my friend. My doctor, he assures me that one dies not of it--and it is for the one time only; you understand, that never--never shall I return.
context: Poirot discusses his dread of the coming sea voyage.

text: To dictate to Hercule Poirot is sheer impertinence. But the sum offered was so stupendous that for the first time in my life I was tempted by mere money. It was a competence--a fortune! And there was a second attraction--*you*, my friend.
context: Poirot explains why he accepted the South American assignment.

text: Only that I fancy that it is something on a large scale. Just a little idea of mine, nothing more. Ah, but I must complete my packing. The time advances.
context: Poirot shares his first intuition about the Big Four.

text: Ah, it is that you do not understand! I have passed my word, you comprehend--the word of Hercule Poirot. Nothing but a matter of life or death could detain me now.
context: Poirot refuses to break his professional commitment.

text: The time, it marches with a rapidity unbelievable. Come now, Hastings, you cannot say that I have left you with nothing to do. A most sensational problem. The man from the unknown. Who is he?
context: Poirot leaves Hastings to investigate the mysterious visitor.

text: But I have been an imbecile. I see clearly at last. It is undoubtedly the blessed saints who stopped the train. Jump, Hastings, but jump, I tell you.
context: Poirot suddenly realizes the journey is a plot to remove him.

text: You do not see? No more did I. But I see now. Hastings, *I was being got out of the way*.
context: Poirot explains why he abandoned the train to Southampton.

text: Those four geniuses who have banded themselves together to work outside the law. A Chinaman, an American, a Frenchwoman, and--another. Pray the good God we arrive back in time, Hastings.
context: Poirot identifies the group behind the attempt to remove him.

text: That is not exactly the point I had in mind, Hastings. If only one window was open it would not intrigue me so much. It is both windows being open that strikes me as curious.
context: Poirot focuses on the unusual state of the windows near Mayerling's body.

text: He has been gagged, Hastings. Gagged and then poisoned.
context: Poirot identifies signs of murder on Mayerling's body.

text: We shall find out nothing. He was killed by inhaling strong prussic acid. It was jammed right under his nose. Then the murderer went away again, first opening all the windows. Hydrocyanic acid is exceedingly volatile, but it has a pronounced smell of bitter almonds.
context: Poirot explains the murder method and the purpose of opening the windows.

text: *Mon ami*, some one has tampered with it. It had still three days to run. It is an eight-day clock, you comprehend?
context: Poirot notices that the stopped clock was deliberately altered.

text: No, no; rearrange your ideas, mon ami. Exercise your little gray cells. You are Mayerling. You hear something, perhaps--and you know well enough that your doom is sealed. You have just time to leave a sign.
context: Poirot guides Hastings through Mayerling's final deduction.

text: Was he, *mon ami*? I think not. He was burly and bluff and red-faced, with a thick moustache and a hoarse voice. He will be none of those things by this time, and for the rest, he has nondescript eyes, nondescript ears, and a perfect set of false teeth. Identification is not such an easy matter as you seem to think.
context: Poirot explains why Number Four's disguise will defeat ordinary identification.

text: It is a duel to the death, *mon ami*. You and I on the one side, the Big Four on the other. They have won the first trick; but they have failed in their plan to get me out of the way, and in the future they have to reckon with Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot declares open opposition to the Big Four.

text: if you wish you may wait in to put salt on the little bird's tail, but for me I do not waste my time so.
context: Poirot dismisses Hastings's hope that the false asylum attendant will return.

text: You talk of evidence, but what evidence have we against him? True, we have a body, but we have no proof even that the man was murdered--prussic acid, when inhaled, leaves no trace. Again, we can find no one who saw any one enter the flat during our absence, and we have found out nothing about the movements of our late friend, Mayerling....
context: Poirot analyzes the lack of usable evidence against Number Four.

text: No, Hastings, Number Four has left no trace, and he knows it. His visit we may call a reconnaissance. Perhaps he wanted to make quite sure that Mayerling was dead, but more likely, I think, he came to see Hercule Poirot, and to have speech with the adversary whom alone he must fear.
context: Poirot interprets Number Four's return as a reconnaissance.

text: And to what end? Can we produce anything to impress a coroner's jury of your solid Britishers? Is our description of Number Four of any value? No; we shall allow them to call it 'Accidental Death,' and may be, although I have not much hope, our clever murderer will pat himself on the back that he deceived Hercule Poirot in the first round.
context: Poirot explains why he will not reveal his theory at the inquest.

text: We go, Hastings, to see the one man in England who knows most of the underground life of China.
context: Poirot tells Hastings the purpose of their journey to see John Ingles.

text: everything is farfetched that comes not from your own imagination; for me, I agree with this gentleman. But continue, I pray, monsieur.
context: Poirot rebukes Hastings and invites Ingles to continue.

text: Not in the least, monsieur. A man took refuge in my rooms. He was suffering badly from shock, but he managed to tell us enough to interest us in this Li Chang Yen. He described four people--the Big Four--an organisation hitherto undreamed of. Number One is Li Chang Yen, Number Two is an unknown American, Number Three an equally unknown Frenchwoman, Number Four may be called the executive of the organisation--*the destroyer*.
context: Poirot summarizes Mayerling's information for John Ingles.

text: Here is where the body lay; that big dark stain and the splashes all around mark the spot. Traces of carpet slippers and 'number nine' boots, you observe, but all very confused. Then two sets of tracks leading to and from the kitchen; whoever the murderer was, he came in that way. You have the boot, Hastings? Give it to me.
context: Poirot reconstructs the movement of the murderer from the footprints.

text: *Mais non*, you do not observe. On one of the outgoing footmarks stained with blood there is superimposed an ingoing one. I wonder what he went back for--the little jade figures as an afterthought? It is all ridiculous--stupid.
context: Poirot corrects Hastings's reading of the overlapping footprints.

text: Very good, Hastings. I wondered whether that hypothesis would occur to you. I had already thought of it and rejected it. Betsy Andrews is a local woman, well-known hereabouts. She can have no connection with the Big Four; and, besides, old Whalley was a powerful fellow, by all accounts.
context: Poirot evaluates and rejects Hastings's theory about Betsy Andrews.

text: Like Jacob's ladder? I know, Hastings, that you have an imagination of the most fertile--but I implore of you to keep it within bounds.
context: Poirot mocks Hastings's elaborate mechanical theory of the murder.

text: How marvellous is my friend Hastings! He knows everything--but everything! How do they say--Inquire Within Upon Everything. That is my friend Hastings.
context: Poirot responds ironically when Hastings explains frozen imported meat.

text: Ah! Monday, is it? A bad day of the week. To commit a murder on a Monday is a mistake.
context: Poirot links the day of the week to the condition of the delivered meat.

text: It is necessary for this reason--Grant is not the murderer.
context: Poirot tells Inspector Meadows why he must interview Grant privately.

text: The murderer was, I should fancy, a youngish man. He drove up to Granite Bungalow in a trap, which he left outside. He went in, committed the murder, came out, and drove away again. He was bare-headed, and his clothing was slightly blood-stained.
context: Poirot states his reconstruction of the killer's arrival and departure.

text: Not with the eyes of the body, perhaps; but with the eyes of the mind, yes.
context: Poirot explains how he inferred the horse and trap without visible tracks.

text: You took them by a kind of reversion to instinct, as it were? You had heard your master say that they were valuable, and you felt you might as well go the whole hog. That, I understand. Now, answer me this. Was it the second time that you went into the room that you took the figures?
context: Poirot uses psychology to obtain the truth from Grant.

text: Ah! That is where the cleverness of Number Four, I am convinced, comes in. Everybody saw him--and yet nobody saw him. You see, he drove up in a butcher's cart!
context: Poirot reveals how the murderer entered the village unnoticed.

text: Have I not saved an innocent man from being sent to the gallows? That is enough for one day.
context: Poirot rebukes Hastings for comparing Number Four's cleverness with his own.

text: He is an artist, this one. He disguises himself not with the false beard and the blue spectacles. He alters his features, yes; but that is the least part. For the time being he *is* the man he would be. He lives in his part.
context: Poirot explains Number Four's method of inhabiting a disguise.

text: we progress. At every contact with this man we learn a little of his mind and his methods. Of us and our plans he knows nothing.
context: Poirot measures their gradual progress against Number Four.

text: *Mon ami*, you do not change. Always the same Hastings, who would be up and at their throats.
context: Poirot teases Hastings for his preference for immediate action.

text: He is known as Number One. Number Two is an American. Number Three is a Frenchwoman. Number Four, the 'Destroyer,' is an Englishman.
context: Poirot describes the known membership of the Big Four to Captain Kent.

text: You reflect not--you use not the little gray cells of the brain. Who are these men who send a portion of your navy to destruction simply as a trial of their power? For that was all it was, Monsieur, a test of this new force of magnetical attraction which they hold.
context: Poirot rebukes Captain Kent for laughing at the scale of the conspiracy.

text: And if it is not work, it is the games, the sport. All those things they take _au grand sérieux. Now, madame, recount to me exactly, in detail, and as methodically as you can, the exact circumstances of your husband's disappearance.
context: Poirot reassures Mrs. Halliday and asks for a methodical account.

text: I wonder where he was that evening. If we knew that, we should know a great deal. Whom did he meet?--there lies the mystery. Madame, myself I do not of necessity accept the view of the police; with them is it always '*Cherchez la femme*.' Yet it is clear that something occurred that night to alter your husband's plans.
context: Poirot identifies the unknown evening meeting as the key to Halliday's disappearance.

text: Well, madame, the solution of the mystery lies in Paris, and to find it I myself journey to Paris on the instant.
context: Poirot decides to pursue the Halliday investigation in Paris.

text: This Big Four, they make me to bestir myself, *mon ami*. I run up and down, all over the ground, like our old friend 'the human foxhound.'
context: Poirot comments on how the Big Four force him into active fieldwork.

text: I devoutly hope not. He loved me not, that one.
context: Poirot reacts to the possibility of meeting Giraud in Paris.

text: Very difficult, *mon ami*. But, as you know well, difficulties rejoice the heart of Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot responds to Hastings's concern about tracing Halliday's movements.

text: *Mais non*, not Madame Olivier. *Cela va sans dire!* There are not many geniuses of her stamp in the world. No, I referred to the other lady--the lady on the stairs.
context: Poirot corrects Hastings about which woman he found unusual.

text: A woman who enters her home--for I presume that it is her home since she enters with a key--and runs straight upstairs without even looking at two strange visitors in the hall to see who they are, is a *very* unusual type of woman--quite unnatural, in fact. *Mille tonnerres!* what is that?
context: Poirot explains the behavioral detail that made the veiled woman suspicious.

text: It was a near thing that! But clumsy, all the same--for I had no suspicion--at least hardly any suspicion. Yes, but for my quick eyes, the eyes of a cat, Hercule Poirot might now be crushed out of existence--a terrible calamity for the world. And you, too, *mon ami*--though that would not be such a national catastrophe.
context: Poirot reacts after a falling tree narrowly misses him and Hastings.

text: We are going to think. Yes, here and now, we are going to exercise our little gray cells. This M. Halliday now, was he really in Paris? Yes, for Professor Bourgoneau, who knows him, saw and spoke to him.
context: Poirot stops to reason through Halliday's verified movements.

text: I see it with the eyes of the mind, *mon ami*. So, and only so, could it have happened. Come, let us go back to the house.
context: Poirot asserts his reconstruction of Halliday's abduction.

text: I propose to enter the villa next door, and release M. Halliday, if he is still alive. I know everything, you see.
context: Poirot confronts the Countess Rossakoff with his plan to rescue Halliday.

text: Her faith in you has never wavered. She is waiting for you--she and the child.
context: Poirot reassures the rescued Halliday about his wife.

text: The Big Four win again. What is that you are holding in your hand, Hastings?
context: Poirot reacts when Halliday refuses to speak about his captors.

text: Admirable, *mon ami*, admirable! Up where, and at whom? Be precise, I beg of you.
context: Poirot challenges Hastings to turn his demand for action into a concrete plan.

text: Wait for them to make a move. See now, in England you all comprehend and adore *la boxe*. If one man does not make a move, the other must, and by permitting the adversary to make the attack one learns something about him. That is our part--to let the other side make the attack.
context: Poirot explains his strategy of waiting for the Big Four to reveal themselves.

text: so it is like that, is it? And what do your friends suggest?
context: Poirot calmly responds when an emissary signals that he represents the Big Four.

text: Subdue your so beautiful and honest nature and sit down. To you, monsieur, I will say this. What is to prevent me ringing up the police and giving you into their custody, whilst my friend here prevents you from escaping?
context: Poirot restrains Hastings and tests the emissary's confidence.

text: 'Will you walk into my parlour, said the spider to the fly?' That is your little English nursery rhyme, is it not? No, no--they are subtle--but not so subtle as Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot recognizes the dropped address as a deliberate trap.

text: My friend, I have been asking myself the reason of this morning's visit. Did our visitor really hope to succeed in bribing me? Or, alternatively, in frightening me into abandoning my task? It seemed hardly credible. Why, then, did he come?
context: Poirot reasons backward from the emissary's implausible purpose.

text: The time, Hastings--the time. If they wanted to decoy me away, surely night time would be better? Why this early hour? Is it possible that something is about to happen this morning? Something which they are anxious Hercule Poirot should not know about?
context: Poirot focuses on the timing of the attempted decoy.

text: I said we were going to leave for England--I did not say we were going to arrive there. Reflect a moment, Hastings. It is quite certain that we are being watched and followed. Our enemies must believe that we are going back to England, and they certainly will not believe that unless they see us get on board the train and start.
context: Poirot explains his plan to fake a genuine departure from Paris.

text: Do not move, I pray of you, madame. You will regret it if you do. Are you acquainted at all with the properties of cuare? The South American Indians use it as an arrow poison. A scratch with it means death.
context: Poirot bluffs Madame Olivier with a cigarette disguised as a blow-pipe.

text: I deserve all that that woman said to me. I am a triple imbecile, a miserable animal, thirty-six times an idiot. I was proud of myself for not falling into their trap. And it was not even meant as a trap--except exactly in the way in which I fell into it. They knew I would see through it--they counted on my seeing through it.
context: Poirot criticizes his own reasoning after escaping Madame Olivier.

text: This gives one furiously to think, does it not?
context: Poirot reacts to Abe Ryland's angry letter.

text: Your bright intellect has grasped the idea, Hastings. Yes, I do. The tone in which you said multi-millionaire was eloquent--but let me impress upon you one fact--this thing is being run by men at the top--and Mr. Ryland has the reputation of being no beauty in his business dealings. An able, unscrupulous man, a man who has all the wealth that he needs, and is out for unlimited power.
context: Poirot explains why he suspects Abe Ryland of being Number Two.

text: My friend, an occasion stupendous, unprecedented, never to be repeated, has presented itself! But there is danger, grave danger. I should not even ask you to attempt it.
context: Poirot proposes that Hastings infiltrate Ryland's household.

text: God guard you, my friend--for I fear that you go into perilous places.
context: Poirot gives Hastings his undercover identity and warns him of the danger.

text: If you fire, you commit murder watched by ten pairs of eyes, and you will be hanged for it. This place is surrounded--has been for the last hour--by Scotland Yard men. It is checkmate, Mr. Abe Ryland.
context: Poirot reveals the police ambush at the quarry.

text: As I have frequently told you, Hastings, you have a nature so beautiful and so honest that unless you are yourself deceived, it is impossible for you to deceive others. Good, then, you are spotted from the first, and they do what I had counted on their doing--a mathematical certainty to any one who uses his gray cells properly--use you as a decoy. They set the girl on--By the way, *mon ami*, as an interesting fact psychologically, has she got red hair?
context: Poirot explains why he kept Hastings ignorant during the infiltration.

text: They are *épatant*--these people! They have even studied your psychology. Oh! yes, my friend, Miss Martin was in the plot--very much so. She repeats the letter to you, together with her tale of Mr. Ryland's wrath, you write it down, you puzzle your brains--the cipher is nicely arranged, difficult, but not too difficult--you solve it, and you send for me.
context: Poirot explains how the conspirators manipulated Hastings into solving the planted cipher.

text: Number Two of the Big Four is Abe Ryland. The masquerading on the part of the footman was to ensure a way of retreat in case of emergencies.
context: Poirot identifies Ryland and explains the purpose of the footman's masquerade.

text: they laugh. That is true, but you have a proverb, have you not: 'He laughs best who laughs at the end'? And at the end, *mon ami*, you shall see.
context: Poirot answers Hastings's complaint that the Big Four are winning.

text: Let us go over all the main facts quietly and methodically. Recount them to me, Hastings, in an orderly and lucid fashion.
context: Poirot asks Hastings to summarize the Yellow Jasmine case.

text: I say to myself, it is a book that talks, not my friend Hastings.
context: Poirot comments ironically on Hastings's formal recital of the facts.

text: *Mon ami*, you and I do not reason in the same way. I am not trying to make up my mind between two opposite solutions--murder or accident--that will come when we have solved the other problem--the mystery of the 'Yellow Jasmine.' By the way, you have left out something there.
context: Poirot distinguishes his reasoning process from Hastings's binary approach.

text: What you think is always so important to yourself, Hastings. But let us pass from the mystery of the Yellow Jasmine to the Mystery of the Curry.
context: Poirot dismisses Hastings's judgment about the dying message.

text: Assuming murder, the question at once arises, why choose that particular method? I can think of only one reason--to confuse identity, the face being charred beyond recognition.
context: Poirot tests a theory about why the victim's face was burned.

text: since there is here something that I do not understand, it would be well that I should investigate the matter. I must not permit myself to be wholly engrossed by the Big Four.
context: Poirot decides to investigate despite uncertainty and his larger obsession.

text: one must not permit oneself to be obsessed by one idea. I have been in danger of that. Figure to yourself, my friend, that even here, in this case, I am in danger of it. Those two lines you mentioned, a downstroke and a line at right angles to it, what are they but the beginning of a 4?
context: Poirot warns himself against forcing every clue into the Big Four theory.

text: Fool that I have been. Triple imbecile! You are a cleverer man than I am, Japp.
context: Poirot realizes from Japp's remark that the victim was already dead when burned.

text: My good Japp, is it possible that you throw the mud in my eyes? I know well enough that it is the Chinaman you suspect. But you are so artful. You want me to help you--and yet you drag the red kipper across the trail.
context: Poirot exposes Japp's attempt to conceal his preferred suspect.

text: Once again, the Big Four. Paynter was a great traveller. In his book there was doubtless some vital information concerning the doings of Number One, Li Chang Yen, the head and brains of the Big Four.
context: Poirot connects the missing manuscript to Li Chang Yen.

text: You always know so much more than I do, Hastings, that it becomes quite fatiguing.
context: Poirot answers Hastings's certainty about Gerald Paynter.

text: To be honest, Hastings, the case is now quite clear to me--all but the words, *Yellow Jasmine*--and I am coming to agree with you that they have no bearing on the crime. In a case of this kind, you have got to make up your mind who is lying. I have done that.
context: Poirot states the remaining uncertainty in the Yellow Jasmine case.

text: Not until I know that all is well--that the arrest is made. Ah! but my psychology has been weak. Hastings, if a man writes a dying message, it is because it is important. Every one has said--'Yellow Jasmine? There is yellow jasmine growing up the house--it means nothing.'
context: Poirot anxiously awaits the arrest after reinterpreting the dying message.

text: My friend, if you were to shoot a man, or stab a man after he were dead, or even knock him on the head, it would be apparent that the injuries were inflicted after death. But with his head charred to a cinder, no one is going to hunt about for obscure causes of death, and a man who has apparently just escaped being poisoned at dinner, is not likely to be poisoned just afterwards. *Who* is lying, that is always the question?
context: Poirot explains why burning the body concealed the true poison death.

text: Because you will not use your little gray cells. Who had a chance to tamper with the curry?
context: Poirot prompts Hastings to reconsider access to the curry.

text: There was no trace of powdered opium in the curry served to Mr. Paynter, but acting in obedience to the suspicions Dr. Quentin had aroused, the old man eats none of it, and preserves it to give to his medical attendant, whom he summons according to plan. Dr. Quentin arrives, takes charge of the curry, *and gives Mr. Paynter an injection*--of strychnine, he says, but really of yellow jasmine--a poisonous dose. When the drug begins to take effect, he departs, after unlatching the window. Then, in the night, he returns by the window, finds the manuscript, and shoves Mr. Paynter into the fire. He does not heed the newspaper that drops to the floor and is covered by the old man's body.
context: Poirot reconstructs Dr. Quentin's poisoning and return to burn the body.

text: The greatest power for evil in the world to-day is this 'Big Four.' To what end they are tending, no one knows, but there has never been another such criminal organisation.
context: Poirot insists to Japp that the Big Four are a major global threat.

text: My poor Japp, your task is not an easy one.
context: Poirot comments on the difficulty of protecting Savaronoff among many visitors.

text: My dear Japp, all through dinner my fingers have been itching to rearrange your own tie-pin. You permit, yes? Ah! that is much more pleasing to the eye. Yes, by all means, let us go to the mortuary.
context: Poirot straightens Japp's tie-pin before agreeing to inspect the body.

text: But no, it was not the rug I was remarking. But it *is* a beautiful specimen, far too beautiful to have a large nail wantonly driven through the middle of it.
context: Poirot explains the detail he noticed in Savaronoff's rug.

text: They seem so, I admit. But one should take no fact for granted until it is proved. Bring me, I pray you, my little scales.
context: Poirot refuses to assume that two ivory bishops are identical.

text: I was right. See you, I was right. Impossible to deceive Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot finds that the two chess pieces have different weights.

text: Wilson was not poisoned, he was electrocuted. A thin metal rod passes up the middle of one of those chessmen. The table was prepared beforehand and set upon a certain spot on the floor. When the bishop was placed upon one of the silver squares, the current passed through Wilson's body, killing him instantly. The only mark was the electric burn upon his hand--his left hand, because he was left-handed.
context: Poirot explains the mechanism that killed Gilmour Wilson.

text: Yes--but there are always two ways of looking at things. Some one gone out--some one come *in*--two totally different things, Hastings. But if you assumed the wrong one, presently some little discrepancy would creep in and show you that you were on the wrong track.
context: Poirot explains how opposite assumptions can fit the same observed event.

text: It means that before two equal deductions I chose the wrong one. You heard me say that it would be easy for any one to impersonate Sonia Daviloff because her uncle had not seen her for so many years?
context: Poirot admits that he chose the wrong of two symmetrical impersonation theories.

text: He enjoys himself as the professor at that match. I doubt not he has visited the chess tourneys to study his part. He sits and frowns in thought; he gives the impression that he is thinking great plans, and all the time he laughs in himself. He is aware that two moves are all that he knows--and all that he *need know*. Again, it would appeal to his mind to foresee the events and to make the man his own executioner at the exact time that suits Number Four....
context: Poirot imagines Number Four's psychology while impersonating Savaronoff.

text: *Mon ami*, he overlooked the little gray cells of Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot names Number Four's decisive mistake.

text: You did, *mon ami*. Since then, you have changed your plan, is it not so?
context: Poirot reminds Hastings that he originally planned only a short stay.

text: Just as I thought. You are a staunch friend, Hastings. It is to serve me that you remain on here. And your wife--little Cinderella as you call her, what does she say?
context: Poirot recognizes that Hastings remains in England out of loyalty.

text: More than you think, my friend. After all, we have established the identity of Number Two and Number Three, and we have learnt more than a little about the ways and methods of Number Four.
context: Poirot summarizes the progress made against the Big Four.

text: You may smile, Hastings--but to penetrate a man's personality, to know exactly what he will do under any given circumstances--that is the beginning of success. It is a duel between us, and whilst he is constantly giving away his mentality to me, I endeavour to let him know little or nothing of mine. He is in the light, I in the shade. I tell you, Hastings, that every day they fear me the more for my chosen inactivity.
context: Poirot explains the psychological purpose of his apparent inactivity.

text: But no! I appeal to your imagination, and you can suggest nothing more subtle than bombs in the fireplace. Well, well, I have need of some matches, I will promenade myself despite the weather. Pardon, my friend, but is it possible that you read *The Future of the Argentine*, *Mirror of Society*, *Cattle Breeding*, *The Clue of Crimson* and *Sport in the Rockies* at one and the same time?
context: Poirot dismisses Hastings's crude threat theory and notices the disordered books.

text: But replace then the others on the bookshelf! Never, never shall I see you embrace the order and the method. Mon Dieu, what then is a bookshelf for?
context: Poirot scolds Hastings for leaving several books out of place.

text: But yes, that was a famous idea of yours--that and the books. See you, if they should say to me any time. 'That friend of yours, that Hastings, he has not the great brain, is it not so?' I shall reply to them: 'You are in error.' It was an idea magnificent and superb that occurred to you there.
context: Poirot praises the warning signs Hastings left in the flat.

text: Am I an imbecile? Of course I understood. It gave me just the warning I needed, and the time to mature my plans. Somehow or other the Big Four had carried you off. With what object?
context: Poirot explains how he interpreted Hastings's warning.

text: You would be used as a decoy to get the great Hercule Poirot into their clutches. I have long been prepared for something of the kind. I make my little preparations, and presently, sure enough, the messenger arrives--such an innocent little street urchin. Me, I swallow everything, and hasten away with him, and, very fortunately, they permit you to come out on the doorstep.
context: Poirot describes how he anticipated the kidnapping trap.

text: If one is prepared in advance all is simple--the motto of the Boy Scout, is it not? And a very fine one. Me, I was prepared.
context: Poirot attributes the rescue to advance preparation.

text: No, no, you do not understand. She is safe and well. She has never been in their hands for one instant.
context: Poirot tells Hastings that his wife was never kidnapped.

text: Well, it did to me. I said nothing to you because I did not want to upset you unnecessarily--but I took measures of my own. Your wife's letters all seem to have been written from the ranch, but in reality she has been in a place of safety devised by me for over three months.
context: Poirot explains the precautions he secretly took for Hastings's wife.

text: You like not that I should embrace you or display the emotion, I know well. I will be very British. I will say nothing--but nothing at all. Only this--that in this last adventure of ours, the honours are all with you, and happy is the man who has such a friend as I have!
context: Poirot restrains his emotion while praising Hastings's courage and loyalty.

text: But surely now we can prepare ourselves for some of their methods of attack now that we are warned of some of our faults. For instance my friend, we know that you should think before you act. Again, if you meet a red-haired young woman in trouble you should eye her--what you say--askance, is it not?
context: Poirot explains how the enemy's character notes can be used defensively.

text: Oh, without doubt, Hastings, in some things they deceive themselves--*tant mieux*! They will learn in due time. Meanwhile we have learnt something, and to know is to be prepared.
context: Poirot answers Hastings's quotation of the enemy's criticisms of him.

text: See you, Hastings, we have to contend against four adversaries; that is, against four different personalities. With Number One we have never come into personal contact--we know him, as it were, only by the impress of his mind--and in passing, Hastings, I will tell you that I begin to understand that mind very well--a mind most subtle and Oriental--every scheme and plot that we have encountered have emanated from the brain of Li Chang Yen.
context: Poirot separates the Big Four into distinct personalities and methods.

text: Number Four succeeds for the opposite reason--he succeeds by the way of obscurity. Who is he? Nobody knows. What does he look like? Again nobody knows.
context: Poirot explains that Number Four's anonymity is his protection.

text: We know that he is a man of medium height, and of medium or fair colouring. If he were a tall man of swarthy complexion he could never have passed himself off as the fair stocky doctor. It is child's play, of course, to put on an additional inch or so for the part of James, or the Professor. In the same way he must have a short straight nose. Additions can be built on to a nose by skilful make up, but a large nose cannot be successfully reduced at a moment's notice.
context: Poirot infers Number Four's natural appearance from the limits of disguise.

text: Nothing alters the face so completely as a different set of teeth. You see where all this is leading us?
context: Poirot identifies false teeth as a central tool in Number Four's disguises.

text: He is a supreme artist in the way he sinks himself in each part he plays.
context: Poirot characterizes Number Four as an actor who disappears into roles.

text: Women are such wonderful observers--they see everything, they notice the little detail that escapes the mere man. I have seen a woman identify one man out of a dozen others--and why, do you think? She had observed that he had a trick of stroking his nose when he was agitated. Now would a man ever have thought of noticing a thing like that?
context: Poirot flatters Flossie Monro to elicit a distinctive habit of Claud Darrell.

text: Yes, *mon ami*. The camera does not lie. One can magnify a photograph, seize salient points that otherwise would remain unnoticed. And then there are a thousand details--such as the structure of the ears, which no one could ever describe to you in words. Oh, yes, it is a great chance, this which has come our way!
context: Poirot explains the evidentiary value of Flossie Monro's photograph.

text: Is it the stars, Hastings? If it is not--if it is not.... Oh, I swear to you, my friend, standing here by this poor woman's body, that I will have no mercy when the time comes!
context: Poirot vows vengeance after Flossie Monro is killed.

text: It is the greatest mistake to underestimate your adversary. Remember that, *mon ami*.
context: Poirot states the principle guiding his campaign against the Big Four.

text: Not exactly. The interview is of my seeking. You may remember my saying that I once did him some small service? He is inclined to be foolishly enthusiastic over my capabilities in consequence, and I am about to trade on this attitude of his.
context: Poirot explains how he obtained an interview with the Home Secretary.

text: That is all I have to say, messieurs--to warn you. I thought it likely that I should not be believed. But at least you will be on your guard. My words will sink in, and each fresh event that comes along will confirm your wavering faith. It was necessary for me to speak now--later I might not have been able to do so.
context: Poirot concludes his warning to the British and French officials.

text: I mean, monsieur, that since I have penetrated the identity of Number Four, my life is not worth an hour's purchase. He will seek to destroy me at all costs--and not for nothing is he named 'The Destroyer.' Messieurs, I salute you. To you, M. Crowther, I deliver this key, and this sealed envelope.
context: Poirot explains the danger to his life and transfers contingency materials.

text: I did not expect to convince Desjardeaux, but I have at least ensured that, if I die, my knowledge does not die with me. And I have made one or two converts. *Pas si mal!*
context: Poirot assesses the results of the official conference.

text: If we were not in the street, I would embrace you.
context: Poirot reacts emotionally to Ingles's decision to go to China.

text: I hope that they will not succeed in massacring Hastings also, that is all. That would annoy me greatly.
context: Poirot dryly expresses concern for Hastings's safety.

text: But there is antimony. And that being the case, we will start immediately for Hertfordshire. Pray Heaven that we are not too late.
context: Poirot reports the soup analysis and decides to act immediately.

text: How many of them were in it, I wonder? I suspect that the whole Templeton family are just so many agents of the Big Four. Did they simply want to decoy us down there? Or was it more subtle than that. Did they intend to play the comedy down there and keep me interested until they had had time to do--what?
context: Poirot analyzes the purpose of the Templeton household's elaborate deception.

text: But yes, but yes, it is indeed I! Not so easy to kill Hercule Poirot!
context: Poirot reveals to Hastings that he survived the explosion.

text: A *ruse de guerre*, my friend, a *ruse de guerre*. All is now ready for our grand coup.
context: Poirot describes his feigned death as a wartime stratagem.

text: No, Hastings, I could not. Never, never, in a thousand years, could you have acted the part at the funeral. As it was, it was perfect. It could not fail to carry conviction to the Big Four.
context: Poirot explains why Hastings could not be told about the feigned death.

text: Do not think me too unfeeling. I carried out the deception partly for your sake. I was willing to risk my own life, but I had qualms about continually risking yours. So, after the explosion, I have an idea of great brilliancy. The good Ridgeway, he enables me to carry it out.
context: Poirot explains that the deception was partly intended to protect Hastings.

text: I was in continual fear of your death lying at my door. And that rendered me nervous--like a cat upon the jumps, as you say. But now I am well satisfied.
context: Poirot explains why hiding Hastings's location has calmed him.

text: And then, *mon ami*, grand resurrection of Hercule Poirot! At the eleventh hour I reappear, throw all into confusion, and achieve the supreme victory in my own unique manner!
context: Poirot describes the planned effect of revealing that he is alive.

text: See you, Hastings, it is like the little trick that you play with the cards. You have seen it without doubt? You take the four knaves, you divide them, one on top of the pack, one underneath, and so on--you cut and you shuffle, and there they are all together again. That is my object.
context: Poirot compares his strategy to gathering four knaves in a card trick.

text: Always impatient, the good Hastings! But no, it will not be so long. The one man they were afraid of--myself--is out of the way. I give them two or three months at most.
context: Poirot predicts how quickly the Big Four will act after his supposed death.

text: Your reasoning is at fault, Hastings. Employ the little gray cells. If your enemies wished to deceive you they would assuredly have seen to it that the Chinaman spoke in intelligible pigeon English. No, the message was genuine.
context: Poirot explains why the dying Chinaman's obscure message was genuine.

text: My dear friend, you *never* see--and anyway the English know no geography.
context: Poirot teases Hastings for failing to understand the Italian words.

text: I am glad that Desjardeaux is convinced at last. *Eh bien*, then, we will start--or rather, I will start. You, Hastings, will remain here--yes, I pray of you. In verity, my friend, I am serious.
context: Poirot tries to leave Hastings behind before the final operation.

text: For you have a part to play, Hastings. An important part! Without you, I might well fail. Nevertheless, I felt that it was my duty to urge you to remain behind.
context: Poirot admits that Hastings is important to the final plan.

text: We are bound, *mon ami* for Karersee, the new Italian name for which is Lago di Carezza. You see now where your 'Cara Zia' comes in and also your 'Carrozza' and 'Largo'--the Handel was supplied by your own imagination. Possibly some reference to the information coming from the 'hand' of M. Ingles started the train of association.
context: Poirot decodes the dying Chinaman's message as the name of the rendezvous.

text: To startle him at a moment when he must act quickly or not at all. And we have this great advantage--he does not know that we recognise him. He thinks that he is safe in his new disguise. How I bless Flossie Monro for telling us of that little habit of his.
context: Poirot explains why he revealed himself to Number Four at dinner.

text: Me, like the great Napoleon, I am on the side of the big battalions.
context: Poirot, disguised as Achille, answers the Countess Rossakoff inside the headquarters.

text: Madame, I did not speak of money. I am a man of intelligence. Nevertheless, this is a true fact--*everyone has his price*. In exchange for life and liberty, I offer you your heart's desire.
context: Poirot, posing as Achille, tries to bargain with the countess for escape.

text: Madame, I--I who speak to you, swear once more. *I will bring the dead to life.*
context: Poirot promises the countess that he can restore her lost child.

text: But yes, my friend, it is I. Brother Achille has gone home again--to the land of myths. It was I all the time. It is not only Number Four who can act a part.
context: Poirot reveals that Achille was his own disguise.

text: Belladona in the eyes, the sacrifice of the moustaches, and a real scar the inflicting of which caused me much pain two months ago--but I could not risk a fake beneath the eagle eyes of Number Four. And the final touch, your own knowledge and belief that there was such a person as Achille Poirot! It was invaluable, the assistance you rendered me, half the success of the coup is due to you!
context: Poirot explains the physical and psychological details of the Achille disguise.

text: And let you go into danger without me by your side? You have a pretty idea of me there! Besides, I always had a hope of finding a way out through the countess.
context: Poirot explains why he did not send a substitute into the headquarters.

text: The countess has a great deal more perspicacity than you have, my dear Hastings. She was taken in at first by my disguise; but she soon saw through it. When she said, 'You are very clever, M. Achille Poirot,' I knew that she had guessed the truth. It was then or never to play my trump card.
context: Poirot explains how he recognized that the countess had penetrated his disguise.

text: But yes! You know my motto--Be prepared. As soon as I found that the Countess Rossakoff was mixed up with the Big Four, I had every possible inquiry made as to her antecedents. I learnt that she had had a child who was reported to have been killed, and I also found that there were discrepancies in the story which led me to wonder whether it might not, after all, be alive.
context: Poirot explains how advance inquiries produced leverage with the countess.

text: And so, when the time came, I had my little *coup de théâtre* all ready!
context: Poirot describes the prepared revelation of the countess's living child.

text: It was fated that he and I should never meet in the flesh. When he received the news of the disaster here, he took the simplest way out. A great brain, my friend, a great brain. But I wish I had seen the face of the man who was Number Four.... Supposing that, after all--but I romance.
context: Poirot reflects on Li Chang Yen's death and his uncertainty about Number Four.

text: Yes, *mon ami*, together we have faced and routed the Big Four; and now you will return to your charming wife, and I--I shall retire. The great case of my life is over. Anything else will seem tame after this. No, I shall retire. Possibly I shall grow vegetable marrows!
context: Poirot announces retirement after defeating the Big Four.

text: They have a good sale always, so I am told. Now why is that, eh, Mademoiselle? I ask it of you as a student of human nature--why should that be?
context: Poirot asks Katherine why detective stories are popular.

text: Sometimes, Mademoiselle! Sometimes! I who speak to you--they have happened to *me*.
context: Poirot contradicts Katherine's belief that crimes happen only in fiction.

text: It is my fancy, perhaps, but I think that you have a yearning in you for interesting happenings. *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, all through my life I have observed one thing--'All one wants one gets!' Who knows?
context: Poirot reads Katherine's appetite for adventure and reflects on desire.

text: It is true that I have the habit of being always right--but I do not boast of it. Good-night, Mademoiselle, and may you sleep well.
context: Poirot jokingly denies being a prophet before bidding Katherine good night.

text: I remember meeting you once, M. Caux, at the *Sûreté* in Paris, though doubtless you have forgotten me?
context: Poirot politely reminds Commissary Caux of an earlier meeting.

text: I came to see if I might be of any assistance?
context: Poirot offers his assistance after learning of the murder.

text: But she talked to you, did she not?
context: Poirot prompts Katherine to describe her impression of the victim.

text: Be brave, Mademoiselle; look at her well. Are you sure that this is the woman you talked to in the train yesterday?
context: Poirot steadies Katherine before she identifies the body.

text: You are an excellent witness, Mademoiselle. There is, then, no question as to the identity, but it is strange, all the same.
context: Poirot praises Katherine's careful identification while noting an anomaly.

text: If she had been struck down, it would have been comprehensible, but the man who strangled her slipped up behind and caught her unawares. A little choke--a little gurgle--that is all that would be heard, and then afterwards--that smashing blow on her face. Now why? Did he hope that if the face were unrecognizable she might not be identified? Or did he hate her so much that he could not resist striking that blow even after she was dead?
context: Poirot analyzes the strangling and the blow that disfigured the victim.

text: What is important? What is not? One cannot say at this stage. But we must note each little fact carefully.
context: Poirot explains why every small fact must be recorded early in an inquiry.

text: This shall be a *'roman policier' à nous*. We will investigate this affair together.
context: Poirot invites Katherine to investigate the case with him.

text: That is so, Monsieur. Now I enjoy the world.
context: Poirot explains his retirement to Van Aldin.

text: That is very well said, M. Van Aldin. I place myself entirely at your service.
context: Poirot accepts Van Aldin's request for help.

text: M. Van Aldin, we are all sensible, deeply sensible, of the pain it must give you to speak of these matters, but believe me, Monsieur, it is not the time for concealments. If justice is to be done, we must know everything. If you will reflect a little minute you will realize the truth of that clearly for yourself.
context: Poirot urges Van Aldin to disclose painful facts for the sake of justice.

text: There are some very famous rubies, originally part of the Crown jewels of Russia; they are unique in character, and their value is almost fabulous. There has been a rumour that they have lately passed into the possession of an American. Are we right in concluding, Monsieur, that you were the purchaser?
context: Poirot identifies the historical rubies and asks whether Van Aldin bought them.

text: There is always a pretty formidable crowd on the track of jewels such as these.
context: Poirot notes that news of famous jewels attracts professional thieves.

text: Let us arrange our facts with order and precision. According to our present theory, this is how they run. The Comte de la Roche knows of your purchase of these jewels. By an easy stratagem he induces Madame Kettering to bring the stones with her. He, then, is the man Mason saw in the train at Paris.
context: Poirot lays out the current theory in orderly form.

text: Madame is surprised to see him, but she deals with the situation promptly. Mason is got out of the way; a dinner basket is ordered. We know from the conductor that he made up the berth for the first compartment, but he did not go into the second compartment, and that a man could quite well have been concealed from him.
context: Poirot reconstructs how the supposed visitor could remain hidden on the train.

text: So far the Comte could have been hidden to a marvel. No one knows of his presence on the train except Madame; he has been careful that the maid did not see his face. All that she could say is that he was tall and dark. It is all most conveniently vague.
context: Poirot highlights the convenient vagueness in the maid's description.

text: I mean we are all agreed on one point, and that is that the Comte de la Roche knows one subject *à fond*: Women. How was it that, knowing women as he does, he did not foresee that Madame would have kept that letter?
context: Poirot questions whether a man experienced with women would overlook a saved letter.

text: *Ah, ça par exemple*, that is of no importance whatever. Naturally, if he committed the crime he will have an alibi. A man with the Comte's experience does not neglect to take precautions. No, I said *if* for a very different reason.
context: Poirot dismisses the importance of an alibi for an experienced criminal.

text: The psychology is at fault. The Comte is a scoundrel--yes. The Comte is a swindler--yes. The Comte preys upon women--yes. He proposes to steal Madame's jewels--again yes.
context: Poirot separates the Comte's known vices from the psychology of murder.

text: Is he the kind of man to commit murder? I say *no*! A man of the type of the Comte is always a coward; he takes no risks. He plays the safe, the mean, what the English call the low-down game; but murder, a hundred times no!
context: Poirot argues that the Comte's cowardly temperament is inconsistent with murder.

text: It is my business to know things, The Comte is a man of intelligence. He is at present at a villa he has leased, the Villa Marina at Antibes.
context: Poirot reveals that he already knows where the Comte is staying.

text: What will you; it is my little hobby, to know things. Naturally I have the time to indulge it. I am not overburdened with affairs.
context: Poirot modestly calls gathering information his hobby.

text: I arrange the facts, that is all.
context: Poirot says he is merely arranging the facts rather than accusing anyone.

text: Monsieur, if a doctor walks along the street and an accident happens, does he say, 'I have retired from my profession, I will continue my walk,' when there is some one bleeding to death at his feet? If I had been already in Nice, and the police had sent to me and asked me to assist them, I should have refused. But this affair, the good God thrust it upon me.
context: Poirot explains why he assists when a case is placed directly before him.

text: I accept. But in that case, I must have frank answers to my questions.
context: Poirot accepts Van Aldin as a client but demands complete frankness.

text: The dancer. Ah-ha! And Madame Kettering objected? Was she very devoted to her husband?
context: Poirot asks whether Ruth's objection arose from devotion to her husband.

text: It was not her heart that suffered, it was her pride--is that what you would say?
context: Poirot distinguishes wounded pride from wounded affection.

text: And now he has inherited two million pounds! *La vie*--it is a strange thing, is it not?
context: Poirot reflects ironically on Kettering's sudden inheritance.

text: I reflect, I speak the philosophy. But to return to where we were. Surely M. Kettering did not propose to allow himself to be divorced without making a fight for it?
context: Poirot returns from philosophizing to the practical question of resistance to divorce.

text: If you do not tell me the truth, I can do nothing.
context: Poirot refuses to proceed while Van Aldin withholds the truth.

text: I think you do. You may rest assured, M. Van Aldin, that I know how to be discreet.
context: Poirot reassures Van Aldin that he can be discreet.

text: And to-day, you are here with Hercule Poirot. How one mounts in the world!
context: Poirot jokes with George about the prestige of serving him.

text: A *morceau* of *filet de sole à la Jeannette* alighted there when I was lunching at the Ritz last Tuesday.
context: Poirot objects to a tiny stain on his waistcoat.

text: Supposing, my good Georges, that you had been born in the same social sphere as your late master, Lord Edward Frampton--that, penniless yourself, you had married an extremely wealthy wife, but that that wife proposed to divorce you, with excellent reasons, what would you do about it?
context: Poirot asks George how an aristocrat might resist a justified divorce.

text: Would he not, Georges? I wonder now. Well, perhaps you are right.
context: Poirot questions George's faith in aristocratic restraint.

text: There is a question I would like to put to Mademoiselle, if I may?
context: Poirot politely asks permission to question Ada Mason.

text: An excess of caution is suspicious, eh?
context: Poirot comments that excessive caution can itself be suspicious.

text: It is a very pretty little fortune that you inherit there, a very pretty little fortune.
context: Poirot emphasizes the fortune Derek inherits.

text: My name is Hercule Poirot, and I am probably the greatest detective in the world. You are quite sure that you did not see or speak to your wife on that train?
context: Poirot introduces himself to Derek with characteristic confidence.

text: *Then how did he know about them?*
context: Poirot asks how Derek knew about the rubies when no one had told him.

text: That is Mademoiselle Grey who speaks? *Bon.* Mademoiselle, I have a word for you from M. Van Aldin, the father of Madame Kettering. He wishes very much to speak with you, either at the Villa Marguerite or at his hotel, whichever you prefer.
context: Poirot telephones Katherine on Van Aldin's behalf.

text: This is our own Roman Policier, is it not?
context: Poirot reminds Katherine that he keeps his promises about their shared investigation.

text: I did not say so. And you, Mademoiselle, what do you think?
context: Poirot refuses to equate the official theory with his own belief.

text: Ah! Very good, you agree with me; that is just what I have said.
context: Poirot warmly approves Katherine's psychological judgment and changes the subject to Derek.

text: It is like this, you see, the Comte de la Roche has an alibi. An alibi, it is a very pestilential thing, and always open to the gravest suspicion. But here we are!
context: Poirot calls an alibi a troublesome and suspicious thing.

text: I look very English, do I not?
context: Poirot greets Katherine while displaying his English clothes.

text: You mock yourself at me, but no matter. Papa Poirot, he always laughs the last.
context: Poirot accepts Katherine's teasing and boasts that he laughs last.

text: You are wrong, in these matters one needs not energy but finesse. At the tennis one meets every one. That is so important. Ah, there is Mr. Kettering.
context: Poirot contrasts energy with finesse and explains why social observation matters.

text: It is amazing, M. Kettering, how well you speak the French, so well that you could be taken for a Frenchman if you chose. That is a very rare accomplishment among Englishmen.
context: Poirot remarks on Derek's unusually fluent French.

text: It was Mademoiselle here who mentioned having seen you going in.
context: Poirot apologetically reveals that Katherine saw Derek enter the compartment.

text: Do not enrage yourself, I beg of you. Every one has his own methods. Me, I have always found it a delightful and pleasing idea to combine business and pleasure together.
context: Poirot defends combining business with pleasure.

text: As you say--a Greek. He is a dealer in antiques of world-wide reputation. He has a small shop in Paris, and he is suspected by the police of being something more.
context: Poirot identifies Papopolous as an antique dealer suspected of receiving stolen goods.

text: I ask myself, I, Hercule Poirot ask myself *why is M. Papopolous suddenly come to Nice?*
context: Poirot dramatically asks why Papopolous has suddenly come to Nice.

text: By the roadside, no. *Ça n'est pas pratique.* But listen now--me, I have made a little suggestion to M. Carrège. He is graciously pleased to approve of it. In each Bureau de Poste in the neighbourhood it has been seen to that there is some one who knows the Comte de la Roche by sight. Because, you see, Monsieur, the best way of hiding a thing is by sending it away by the post.
context: Poirot explains why posting an object is safer than hiding it nearby.

text: Order, method, being prepared for eventualities beforehand--that is all there is to it.
context: Poirot attributes his success to order, method, and preparation.

text: Grant me a little moment, Monsieur. Me, I have a little idea. Many people have mocked themselves at the little ideas of Hercule Poirot--and they have been wrong.
context: Poirot asks for time and defends the value of his little ideas.

text: You are shrewd, M. Papopolous. No, it is not. They are not, for instance, worth five hundred thousand dollars.
context: Poirot tells Papopolous the displayed rubies are not worth the supposed fortune.

text: *I am not concerned with the recovery of these jewels. That is the affair of the police.* I am working not for the police but for M. Van Aldin. I want to lay hands on the man who killed Madame Kettering. I am interested in the jewels only in so far as they may lead me to the man.
context: Poirot states that he seeks the murderer rather than the stolen jewels.

text: But to continue. When a Russian Grand Duke, an Austrian Archduchess, or an Italian Prince wish to dispose of their family jewels--to whom do they go? To M. Papopolous, is it not? He who is famous all over the world for the discretion with which he arranges these things.
context: Poirot flatters Papopolous while pressing him for help.

text: I think there is. You may remember, M. Papopolous, that seventeen years ago there was a certain article in your hands, left there as security by a very--er--Prominent Person. It was in your keeping and it unaccountably disappeared. You were, if I may use the English expression, in the soup.
context: Poirot invokes an old debt to persuade Papopolous to share information.

text: Seventeen years is a long time, but I believe that I am right in saying, Monsieur, that your race does not forget.
context: Poirot appeals to Papopolous's long memory and sense of obligation.

text: You and your father will perhaps dine with me one night.
context: Poirot gallantly assures Zia that age has not changed her and invites her to dinner.

text: I am always punctual, The exactitude--always do I observe it.
context: Poirot praises punctuality, order, and method.

text: I have come to ask you yet more questions, We must get to the bottom of this affair. Always I return to the question of the man in the train. You have been shown the Comte de la Roche. You say that it is possible he was the man, but you are not sure.
context: Poirot returns methodically to the uncertain identification of the man on the train.

text: Just so, Mademoiselle, but reflect a minute. The train has just arrived at the Gare de Lyon. Many of the passengers promenade themselves upon the quay. Your mistress was about to do so, and for that purpose had doubtless put on her fur coat, eh?
context: Poirot reconstructs why Derek might have been wearing an overcoat and hat at the station.

text: Your master, then, does the same. The train is heated, but outside in the station it is cold. He puts on his overcoat and his hat and he walks along beside the train, and looking up at the lighted windows he suddenly sees Madame Kettering. Until then he has had no idea that she was on the train. Naturally, he mounts the carriage and goes to her compartment.
context: Poirot completes a plausible reconstruction of Derek discovering his wife on the train.

text: You ask me a question; I answer it. You say have I not shown you the jewels yesterday? I reply--no. What I showed you, M. Van Aldin, was a first-class imitation, hardly to be distinguished except by an expert from the real ones.
context: Poirot explains that the jewels he displayed were only an expert imitation.

text: Certainly. There is no doubt as to that. See, I will recount to you the affair as I see it. The Comte knew of the rubies and he laid his plans accordingly.
context: Poirot begins reconstructing the Comte's intended jewel substitution.

text: He made up a romantic story of a book he was writing, so as to induce your daughter to bring them with her. He provided himself with an exact duplicate. It is clear, is it not, that substitution is what he was after.
context: Poirot explains the Comte's planned substitution of imitation rubies.

text: Then you happened to mention that you had once mistaken the Comte for your son-in-law. That told me that they were of the same height and build, and alike in colouring. It put some curious ideas in my head. The maid had only been with your daughter a short time. It was unlikely that she would know Mr. Kettering well by sight, since he had not been living in Curzon Street; also the man was careful to keep his face turned away.
context: Poirot explains why Derek's resemblance to the Comte and the maid's unfamiliarity mattered.

text: No, no, I did not say that--but it is a possibility--a very strong possibility. He was in a tight corner, a very tight corner, threatened with ruin. This was the one way out.
context: Poirot carefully distinguishes suspicion from a proven accusation.

text: Leave it in the hands of Hercule Poirot, have no fears. I will discover the truth.
context: Poirot grandly assures Van Aldin that he will discover the truth.

text: You will pardon an old man, Monsieur, if he says to you something that you may consider impertinent. There is one of your English proverbs that I would quote to you. It says that 'it is well to be off with the old love, before being on with the new.'
context: Poirot warns Derek with an English proverb about ending one romance before beginning another.

text: I would not say that. Your reputation is bad, yes, but with women--never does that deter them. If you were a man of excellent character, of strict morality who had done nothing that he should not do, and--possibly everything that he should do--*eh bien!* then I should have grave doubts of your success. Moral worth, you understand, it is not romantic. It is appreciated, however, by widows.
context: Poirot argues that moral perfection is less romantic to women than a bad reputation.

text: The personality of a criminal, Georges, is an interesting matter. Many murderers are men of great personal charm.
context: Poirot reflects that many murderers possess great personal charm.

text: I shall go to bed and take a *tisane*. The expected has happened, and when the expected happens, it always causes me emotion.
context: Poirot reacts to an expected development by retiring with a tisane.

text: And so it is, that we are the good friends and have no secrets from each other.
context: Poirot asserts friendship and mutual confidence with Katherine.

text: *Ah, mais c'est Anglais ça*, everything in black and white, everything clear cut and well defined. But life, it is not like that, Mademoiselle. There are the things that are not yet, but which cast their shadow before.
context: Poirot contrasts English clarity with life's ambiguous shadows.

text: If you had said in an indifferent voice, 'Oh, quite nice,' *eh bien*, do you know I should have been better pleased.
context: Poirot reads emotional significance into Katherine's enthusiastic answer about Knighton.

text: You do not see why I am being so impertinent, Mademoiselle? I am an old man, and now and then--not very often--I come across some one whose welfare is dear to me. We are friends, Mademoiselle. You have said so yourself. And it is just this--I should like to see you happy.
context: Poirot explains that friendship makes Katherine's happiness important to him.

text: Perhaps you are right, Mademoiselle. See you, I who speak to you have seen much of the world, and I know that there are two things which are true. A good man may be ruined by his love for a bad woman--but the other way holds good also. A bad man may equally be ruined by his love for a good woman.
context: Poirot reflects that love can ruin good and bad people alike.

text: There are others--more dangerous than the Comte de la Roche. They have qualities that appeal--recklessness, daring, audacity. You are fascinated, Mademoiselle; I see that, but I think that it is no more than that. I hope so.
context: Poirot warns Katherine about the fascination of reckless and audacious men.

text: You could, perhaps, love a thief, Mademoiselle, *but not a murderer*.
context: Poirot draws a moral boundary between loving a thief and loving a murderer.

text: M. Van Aldin, if I may say so, behaved exceedingly foolishly.
context: Poirot agrees that Van Aldin was foolish to refuse Mirelle an interview.

text: M. Van Aldin is an obstinate man, I do not argue with obstinate men. I act in spite of them. We will go and see the lady immediately. I will tell her that you are empowered by M. Van Aldin to act for him, and you will guard yourself well from contradicting me.
context: Poirot says he acts around obstinate men rather than arguing with them.

text: He is indisposed, the Riviera throat, it has him in its grip, but me, I am empowered to act for him, as is Major Knighton, his secretary. Unless, of course, Mademoiselle would prefer to wait a fortnight or so.
context: Poirot invents a polite illness to make Mirelle speak immediately.

text: It is necessary to act while--how do you say it?--the iron is hot, She is temperamental; in an hour's time, maybe, she will repent, and she will wish to draw back. We must prevent that at all costs.
context: Poirot explains why a temperamental witness must be taken to the magistrate at once.

text: I was not aware that the fact had been mentioned outside the Examining Magistrate's office.
context: Poirot catches Mirelle referring to confidential knowledge.

text: On the contrary, I think it agrees with them very well. Au revoir, Messieurs; if news of any importance comes to me I will communicate it to you immediately.
context: Poirot says new information about the Marquis fits his private ideas.

text: I have been the cat, watching at the mouse hole. I have been the good dog following up the scent, and not taking my nose from the trail. And also, my good Georges, I have been the squirrel. I have stored away the little fact here, the little fact there. I go now to my store and I take out one particular nut, a nut that I stored away--let me see, seventeen years ago.
context: Poirot compares his method of storing facts to a squirrel collecting nuts.

text: See, I admit it frankly. As a rule I know everything, but in this case, I--well, I do not. You, perhaps, know better than I do.
context: Poirot candidly admits uncertainty about Katherine's feelings.

text: Mademoiselle, what you have said there is very just--very true. I was struggling in darkness, and you have shown me a light. There was a point that puzzled me and you have made it plain.
context: Poirot tells Lenox that her idea has illuminated a point that puzzled him.

text: But I will tell you this, Mademoiselle. I am not satisfied; no, I, Hercule Poirot, am not yet satisfied. It may be that this very night I shall learn something more. At least, I go to try.
context: Poirot declares that he remains unsatisfied despite Derek's arrest.

text: Some one who might know something. In these matters one must leave no stone unturned. Au revoir, Mademoiselle.
context: Poirot says every possible source of information must be pursued.

text: You are the daughter of your father, Mademoiselle Zia. To know when to stop. Ah! that is the art.
context: Poirot praises Zia for knowing when to stop gambling.

text: So it is said. Men are foolish, are they not, Mademoiselle? To eat, to drink, to breathe the good air, it is a very pleasant thing, Mademoiselle. One is foolish to leave all that simply because one has no money--or because the heart aches. *L'amour*, it causes many fatalities, does it not?
context: Poirot reflects that life is worth keeping despite poverty or heartbreak.

text: When I look at you, it seems much less, You were then very much as you are now, Mademoiselle, a little thinner, a little paler, a little more serious. Sixteen years old and fresh from your pension. Not quite the *petite pensionnaire*, not quite a woman. You were very delicious, very charming, Mademoiselle Zia; others thought so too, without doubt.
context: Poirot gallantly recalls Zia as a sixteen-year-old girl.

text: That may be, yes, that well may be. At sixteen one is credulous, is one not? One believes what one is told.
context: Poirot observes that youth is credulous.

text: I guessed. It was a good guess, was it not, Mademoiselle? You see, unless you are good at guessing, it is not much use being a detective.
context: Poirot explains that his detective success came from a good guess.

text: But no, but no! Debarrass yourself of that idea, Mademoiselle. I am not a blackmailer. I do not hold your secret over your head and threaten you with it.
context: Poirot assures Zia that he will not blackmail her with an old secret.

text: Listen, and I will tell you why. Women, Mademoiselle, are generous. If they can render a service to one who has rendered a service to them, they will do it. I was generous once to you, Mademoiselle. When I might have spoken, I held my tongue.
context: Poirot appeals to generosity and reciprocity rather than coercion.

text: You are young, Mademoiselle, but there are three things that cannot be hurried--*le bon Dieu*, Nature, and old people.
context: Poirot says that God, nature, and old people cannot be hurried.

text: *Ma foi*, I needed it! I am now a lion--a giant. Ah, Mademoiselle Katherine, you have not seen me as I can be. You have seen the gentle, the calm Hercule Poirot; but there is another Hercule Poirot. I go now to bully, to threaten, to strike terror into the hearts of those who listen to me.
context: Poirot theatrically contrasts his gentle manner with his intimidating investigative side.

text: Permit me to say that you have been badly educated. It is the name of one of the great ones of this world.
context: Poirot rebukes the servants for not recognizing his famous name.

text: Ah, yes; on seven occasions at least. I will recite them to you.
context: Poirot calmly lists the servants' previous lies.

text: But it is not of these past lapses that I wish to speak, only, my dear friend, do not get into the habit of thinking yourself too clever. I come now to the particular lie in which I am concerned--your statement that the Comte de la Roche arrived at this villa on the morning of 14th January.
context: Poirot warns a servant not to think himself too clever before naming the relevant lie.

text: Odd, how one remembers some things--and forgets others.
context: Poirot points out the selective nature of memory.

text: Yes, yes, it is as I say. You tell your lies and you think nobody knows. But there are two people who know. Yes--two people. One is *le bon Dieu*--
context: Poirot says both God and another observer know when people lie.

text: And the other is Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot names himself as the other witness to their lies.

text: Am I to stop here all day, arguing with a couple of imbeciles thus? It is the truth I want. If you will not give it to me, that is your look out. *For the last time, when did Monsieur le Comte arrive at the Villa Marina--Tuesday morning or Wednesday morning?*
context: Poirot angrily demands the truth about the Comte's arrival date.

text: I tell you I know what happened as well as though I had been there. You went into her compartment and you found her dead. I tell you I know it. To lie to me is dangerous. Be careful, Mademoiselle Mirelle.
context: Poirot warns Mirelle that lying to him is dangerous because he knows what happened.

text: I said to myself, 'If you want to know anything about the dramatic profession there is one person who knows all that is to be known and that is my old friend, Mr. Joseph Aarons.'
context: Poirot flatters theatrical agent Joseph Aarons before seeking information.

text: *Précisément.* Now I want to ask you, Monsieur Aarons, what you know about a young woman called Kidd.
context: Poirot precisely names Kitty Kidd as the woman he is tracing.

text: A ruby the size of a pigeon's egg!
context: Poirot reacts keenly to news of Mirelle's enormous ruby.

text: But if I remember rightly, the ruby that is named 'Heart of Fire' is the centre stone in a necklace.
context: Poirot recalls that the Heart of Fire belongs at the center of a necklace.

text: No, no--somehow I do not think it is coloured glass.
context: Poirot quietly rejects the idea that Mirelle's stone is merely glass.

text: Yes, you are older. And by that I do not mean that the wrinkles and the crows' feet are coming. When I first saw you, Mademoiselle, you were a looker-on at life. You had the quiet, amused look of one who sits back in the stalls and watches the play.
context: Poirot tells Katherine that she has changed from an observer into a participant.

text: Now, you no longer watch. It is an absurd thing, perhaps, that I say here, but you have the wary look of a fighter who is playing a difficult game.
context: Poirot describes Katherine as a wary fighter in a difficult game.

text: You have heard me speak of my friend Hastings?--he who said that I was a human oyster. *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, I have met my match in you. You, far more than I, play a lone hand.
context: Poirot compares Katherine's reserve with Hastings's description of him as a human oyster.

text: Never does Hercule Poirot talk nonsense. It is as I say.
context: Poirot insists that he never talks nonsense.

text: I see that that tells you nothing. But I will not be a human oyster. No, I will lay my cards on the table, which is assuredly a thing that oysters do not do. You suspect, do you not, that I am not satisfied with the case against Derek Kettering?
context: Poirot says he will lay his cards on the table instead of behaving like an oyster.

text: *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, what I have done I do not regret. I have only one duty--to discover the truth, and that way led straight to Mr. Kettering. But did it end there? The police say yes, but I, Hercule Poirot, am not satisfied.
context: Poirot explains that truth, not the police's conclusion, is his only duty.

text: I have asked myself, Mademoiselle, a hundred times, 'Was Derek Kettering the kind of man who would deal that smashing blow after having committed the murder?' What end would it serve? What purpose would it accomplish? Was it a likely action for one of Monsieur Kettering's temperament? And, Mademoiselle, the answer to these questions is profoundly unsatisfactory.
context: Poirot repeatedly questions the purpose and psychology of the blow to the victim's face.

text: Yes, as you say, from the Ritz. I am a luxurious person sometimes--when a millionaire pays.
context: Poirot jokes about luxury when a millionaire pays his expenses.

text: No, with the Press--a much more deadly weapon.
context: Poirot calls the press a deadlier threat than the police.

text: A mirror shows the truth, but every one stands in a different place for looking into the mirror.
context: Poirot compares different viewpoints to people looking into the same mirror.

text: Courage, Mademoiselle, you must not fail now; the end is very near.
context: Poirot urges Katherine to keep courage because the end is near.

text: No, no, *pas la guerre*--a memory of my days in the police force, a bullet of a rascally Apache.
context: Poirot dismisses concern about an old bullet wound with a French phrase.

text: I am silent, but all the same I say it is a pity. And now, Messieurs, let us come to business.
context: Poirot ends a personal digression and turns the men back to business.

text: I am not mad. I am eccentric, perhaps--at least certain people say so; but as regards my profession, I am very much, as one says, 'all there.' I ask you, Monsieur Van Aldin, whether you would be glad or sorry if what I tell you should be the case?
context: Poirot distinguishes eccentricity from professional madness and asks Van Aldin to face his feelings.

text: I have my own methods. The exercise of a little tact, a little cleverness--and the thing is done.
context: Poirot attributes his success in breaking an alibi to tact and cleverness.

text: It is as yet only a theory, but I tell you this, Monsieur Van Aldin, the facts are worth investigating. You must come out with me to the south of France and go into the case on the spot.
context: Poirot calls his new idea a theory worth testing on the scene.

text: *Mon Dieu*, but that is something I have not thought of! We must break our journey in Paris. Quick, quick, let us alight at once.
context: Poirot suddenly insists on breaking the journey in Paris.

text: *Ma foi*, I lose my head nowadays. Let us return and continue our journey quietly. With reasonable luck the train will not have gone.
